items.title: India has the world's largest emigrant population, with over 18.5 million people of Indian origin living overseas in 2024, accounting for 6% of all in...
items.description_html:
India has the world's largest emigrant population, with over 18.5 million people of Indian origin living overseas in 2024, accounting for 6% of all international migrants globally.

According to the United Nations, an international migrant is someone who has moved across an international border and changed their usual place of residence, typically living in the new country continuously for at least 12 months.

A significant portion of India's out-migrant population is the historical legacy of the Partition of 1947. However, as the Partition generation ages and dies out, this number has been declining. Pakistan is still home to 1.6 million Indian-born individuals, making up nearly 9% of all Indians living abroad in 2024.

Outside of Pakistan, Indian emigration remains concentrated within Asia, particularly in West Asia, where half of all Indian emigrants live. Additionally, one-quarter of Indian out-migrants live in North America, a share that has grown over time, as has emigration to Australia and New Zealand.

How do emigration patterns differ between Indian men and women? Read @nileena_suresh's analysis to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/international-migration/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Migration #Immigration #Emigration #India #DataForIndia

items.title: One-third of cars on the road in Norway are now electric— Norway is leading the way in the transition from petrol to electric cars. Almost every new ...
items.description_html:
One-third of cars on the road in Norway are now electric—

Norway is leading the way in the transition from petrol to electric cars. Almost every new car sold in Norway is electric. Hardly anyone buys a combustion engine car anymore.

However, data on new car sales doesn’t tell us about the distribution of cars on the road. There is a lag between sales and stocks, because people can hold on to their existing petrol and diesel cars for as much as a decade or more.

But after years of electric cars dominating the market, one-third of cars in use in Norway are now electric. The chart shows this growth.

The share was only 12% five years earlier, which shows that this transition can happen relatively quickly.

As the global leader, Norway’s experiences can help to inform other countries on factors like charging networks, grid management, and the impacts of electric car uptake on emissions and air quality.

(This Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie.)

items.title: With over 1.3 billion people, India is the most populous country in the world. However, its population growth has been gradually slowing down. There w...
items.description_html:
With over 1.3 billion people, India is the most populous country in the world. However, its population growth has been gradually slowing down.

There was certainly a time when India's population was growing very fast. In the three decades after Independence, India's population doubled. But the country’s population growth has slowed down, a trend that began from the 1980s.

By the early 2020s, India's population growth rate is estimated to have fallen below the world average and the gap is expected to grow.

This slowing down of population growth occurred at two distinct speeds across India. Until the 1970s, population growth rates in different states were quite similar. However since the 1980s, India's southern states have been growing far slower than the central, northern and eastern states.

Even with these shifts, India is projected to grow to nearly 1.7 billion people at its peak size, the mid-2060s, and is expected to continue to be the world's most populous country until beyond 2100.

Why is India's population growth slowing down? Read @Rukmini's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/population-growth/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Population #Census #PopulationData #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Propelled by rising mobile phone and internet access, India is witnessing an exponential growth in digital payments. Just two decades ago, more than 9...
items.description_html:
Propelled by rising mobile phone and internet access, India is witnessing an exponential growth in digital payments.

Just two decades ago, more than 95% of the money spent every day on major retail payments was made using cheques. However, since the introduction of the National Electronic Fund Transfer (NEFT) in 2005-06, and later with the rise of UPI, digital retail payments have come to dominate, according to statistics maintained by the Reserve Bank of India.

Digital retail payments, including NEFT, the Immediate Payments Service (IMPS), credit and debit cards, and Unified Payments Interface (UPI), together account for daily transactions of over Rs 2.2 trillion ($25 billion).

In the last five years, UPI has in particular grown dramatically. The average daily number of UPI transactions grew from 40 million to 550 million per day, outstripping all other modes of digital payments.

But has this progress in retail digital payments led to equitable access to financial services across India? Read @akwaghmare’s piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/retail-payments/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Payments #Banking #Finance #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Despite substantial progress in enrolment in school and college, a majority of Indians above the age of 25 are still either illiterate or educated onl...
items.description_html:
Despite substantial progress in enrolment in school and college, a majority of Indians above the age of 25 are still either illiterate or educated only up to the primary level. Just over one in ten, meanwhile, have completed a higher education programme. This data comes from the 2022-23 Periodic Labour Force Survey.

However, these numbers are not uniform across society. Data for different social groups shows that the level of educational attainment of Indians from marginalised communities is the lowest.

The PLFS asks households if they belong to a Scheduled Tribe, Schedule Caste or Other Backward Class, and if not, they are classified as “Others”. The “Others” category is largely composed of people from upper caste groups.

An Indian belonging to a Scheduled Tribe is nearly twice as likely to be illiterate as someone who belongs to an upper caste group. On the other hand, Indians who belong to upper caste groups are twice as likely to have a higher education as those belonging to a Scheduled Caste.

Read Abhishek Waghmare’s piece to learn more about how educational levels change across various socio-economic groups in India: https://www.dataforindia.com/education-levels-in-india/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Education #Literacy #HigherEducation #School #College #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Over the last two decades, India has seen a big push towards financial inclusion. Having a bank account is an important first step in that journey, al...
items.description_html:
Over the last two decades, India has seen a big push towards financial inclusion. Having a bank account is an important first step in that journey, allowing account-holders to receive welfare benefits, save, borrow and invest.

From fewer than half of Indian households having a bank account in 2006, 96% of households had a bank account by 2021, according to the National Family Health Survey.

There are now nearly 2.5 billion bank accounts held by individuals in India, as reported by banks. These numbers can include multiple accounts held by the same person, but exclude institutional accounts such as those owned by corporations or trusts.

Indian men hold 50% more bank accounts than women.

Despite most adults now having a bank account, the extent of usage could still have some way to go. Read @akwaghmare's piece to find out more: https://www.dataforindia.com/access-to-banking/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

items.title: Year 2080. I’m on my deathbed. My room looks exactly the same as it did in 2020 — dull walls, no paint, no upgrades. Why? Because I spent my whole l...
items.description_html:
Year 2080. I’m on my deathbed.

My room looks exactly the same as it did in 2020 — dull walls, no paint, no upgrades. Why?

Because I spent my whole life investing every rupee I earned.
No vacations. No experiences. Just SIPs, stocks, and spreadsheets.

My grandchildren are thrilled with the inheritance. But me?

I never lived the life I was building. I partied in my room. I vacationed in my head.

Don’t be this person.
💡 Invest wisely, but live fully.
✅ Set a fixed % to invest
✅ Enjoy life with the rest

You invest for a better tomorrow. When that tomorrow comes — don’t forget to live it.

Balance matters in life and in investing.

Have a great weekend!

items.title: India’s female labour force participation rate (LFPR) is low compared to global standards, but has risen over the last decade. The LFPR measures the ...
items.description_html:
India’s female labour force participation rate (LFPR) is low compared to global standards, but has risen over the last decade.

The LFPR measures the share of adult Indians who are either working or seeking work.

While most Indian men report being in the labour force, the largest share ofIndian women report their main activity as "engaged in domestic duties"—including housework and care work.

Over the last decade, however, India’s female LFPR has risen significantly, alongside higher rates of enrolment in education. As a result, the share of women who say that their main activity is housework is the lowest it has ever been.

Male LFPR, on the other hand, has gradually fallen since the 1980s, with growing enrolment in education.

What are the women who have moved into the labour force doing? Read @akwaghmare's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/female-lfpr-change/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Work #Women #Employment #LFPR #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Open defecation is a serious challenge to public health and to the dignity of individuals. It is associated with the spread of diseases like diarrhoea...
items.description_html:
Open defecation is a serious challenge to public health and to the dignity of individuals. It is associated with the spread of diseases like diarrhoea, cholera and typhoid, and exposes women and children to unsafe locations and the risk of harassment and assault.

Additionally, the use of unsanitary facilities also affects sanitation workers tasked with handling waste in unsafe conditions.

Under the United Nations' Sustainable Development Goal 6.2, India has committed to eliminating open defecation and providing equitable access to sanitation and hygiene for all by 2030. Administrative data from the Indian government captures the number of toilets built. However, to understand actual usage rather than just access, we look at the most recent data on toilet usage which comes from the 2021 National Family Health Survey.

The proportion of Indian households defecating in the open dropped from 70% in 1993 to 19% by 2021, reflecting major progress. However, challenges remain, as about one in four rural households still did not use a toilet facility.

Over half of the Indian households that were forced to defecate in the open resided in just four states - Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Maharashtra and Tamil Nadu.

What are the causes for these disparities in toilet usage in India? Read @nileena_suresh's piece to find out more: https://www.dataforindia.com/sanitation/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Toilets #Sanitation #WASH #India #DataForIndia

items.title: I'm no expert at public speaking, but I've been part of enough panel discussions to list a few pitfalls we can avoid: 1> If your speaking duration has...
items.description_html:
I'm no expert at public speaking, but I've been part of enough panel discussions to list a few pitfalls we can avoid:

1> If your speaking duration has been shortened, don't spend that precious time saying "...but I don't have enough time". The audience doesn't care. Just get to the point.

2> Never read from a written speech. It disconnects us from the audience. Notes and pointers are okay. Make eye contact with the audience.

3> Your target audience isn't the other panellists. They are just thinking about what they are going to speak. Say something that will be useful to the attendees.

4> No complex paragraphs. Divide your comments into 4 points, 4 misonceptions, 4 trends etc. It's easier for a listener to follow and check further details later.

5> BLUF (Bottom line upfront). At @TakshashilaInst we call it the Reverse Bollywood Format. Make the main argument right upfront. Don't leave it to the "In conclusion.." section.

6> Never ever exceed your time limit. That's the worst form of disrespect in such settings.

7> Even if others before you have eaten up a lot of time, compress your remarks to end on time. The audience will thank you.

items.title: One of the most important measures of a country's nutritional and public health status is whether its children are adequately nourished. The most clos...
items.description_html:
One of the most important measures of a country's nutritional and public health status is whether its children are adequately nourished. The most closely tracked indicator on child nutrition outcomes is stunting.

A child is considered stunted if their height is significantly lower than the average for children of the same age and gender, using World Health Organization growth standards.

In 1993, more than one in two children under the age of five across India was stunted. By 2022, this rate dropped to one in three, indicating a decline in stunting over time.

Even then, the levels of child stunting in India remain high and are comparable to those seen in Sub-Saharan African nations with much lower per capita incomes. Bangladesh too had higher levels of stunting than India in the 1990s, but has lowered the prevalence of stunting among young children much faster in the last two decades than India.

Data on stunting in India comes from the National Family Health Surveys, and for other countries from the Demographic and Health Surveys system.

How does stunting affect children from different regions and income groups across the country? Read @nileena_suresh's analysis to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/child-stunting/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Health #PublicHealth #Child #Stunting #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Higher education is seen as being key to getting young Indians into skilled jobs, and to accelerating economic growth. In India, just under 40 million...
items.description_html:
Higher education is seen as being key to getting young Indians into skilled jobs, and to accelerating economic growth.

In India, just under 40 million young people (age 18-23) were enrolled in higher education programmes as of 2022. This included both undergraduate (or equivalent) and postgraduate (or equivalent) levels of study.

Among the 33 million enrolled in undergraduate programmes, a notable gender gap exists. Male students outnumber females, particularly in Engineering where the ratio is more than two to one.

Additionally, over five million students are enrolled in postgraduate programmes, such as masters degrees. At the postgraduate level there are more female students enrolled, but male students outnumber female students in some courses, like management.

There are stark differences between states in higher education, with richer states having a higher share of graduates in the population. There is also a substantial gender gap; in most Indian states, substantially fewer women than men have a higher education.

Which undergraduate and postgraduate disciplines do female and male students choose to study? Read Abhishek Waghmare’s piece to learn more: https://www.dataforindia.com/higher-education/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#College #Undergraduate #Education #HigherEducation #India #DataForIndia

items.title: India is a relatively young country. Half of India's population is under the age of 30, making it a far younger country than its European, North Ameri...
items.description_html:
India is a relatively young country. Half of India's population is under the age of 30, making it a far younger country than its European, North American and East Asian counterparts. The median American or Chinese person, for instance, is over a decade older than the median Indian.

However, India is ageing. By the mid 2030s, the median Indian is expected to be older than the world’s median. Even within India, there are wide variations between states in the ages of their populations.

States in India's centre-north have historically been poorer with lower levels of education, as a result of which fertility is higher in these states. Higher birth rates - meaning that a larger number of children are born every year - make these states relatively younger. India's richer southern and western states, meanwhile, have lower fertility rates and are, as a result, ageing rapidly.

Kerala, for instance, has twice the share of elderly people (age 60+) that Bihar or Uttar Pradesh have.

items.title: Literacy - the ability to read and write a sentence in any language with proper comprehension - is the most fundamental measure of educational outcome...
items.description_html:
Literacy - the ability to read and write a sentence in any language with proper comprehension - is the most fundamental measure of educational outcomes.

India has made remarkable progress in improving literacy rates, rising from fewer than two in ten literate in the early 1950s, to eight in ten as of 2023. Illiteracy in India is largely a historical legacy, concentrated among older age groups.

India’s literacy data comes from the Census (until 2011) and subsequent nationally representative sample surveys by the National Statistics Office (NSO).

Even with this progress, male literacy rates (86%) far exceed female literacy rates (74%).

Among Indian states, some of India's north-eastern states, as well as wealthier states including Goa and Kerala are approaching the milestone of 100% female literacy. Rajasthan (62%) and Andhra Pradesh (65%), meanwhile, have lowest female literacy rates across India.

How do various marginalised groups fare in terms of literacy? Read @akwaghmare's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/literacy-in-india/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Literacy #Education #LivingConditions #India #DataForIndia

items.title: The last two decades have seen a massive increase in the number of mobile phone connections in India. 85% of Indian adults, or about 850 million peopl...
items.description_html:
The last two decades have seen a massive increase in the number of mobile phone connections in India. 85% of Indian adults, or about 850 million people, had access to a mobile phone as of 2023.

The rise of mobile phones also powered the spread of internet availability in India. Only 2% of homes in India had internet access in 2009, but once mobile broadband entered the space, the pace of access accelerated. By 2014, nearly three in ten homes had internet access and by 2023, about eight in ten homes were connected to the internet.

Although phones have become the primary way of accessing the internet for Indians, not everyone with a mobile phone uses the internet. More than a third of women who use phones do not use the internet, and more women than men have neither used the phone nor the internet.

@akwaghmare decodes access to phones and internet in India, read his analysis to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/comm-tech/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Mobile #Phone #Internet #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Road accidents are a leading cause of mortality in India. 36 out of every 1,000 deaths in India in 2019 were attributed to road accidents. Among young...
items.description_html:
Road accidents are a leading cause of mortality in India. 36 out of every 1,000 deaths in India in 2019 were attributed to road accidents. Among young men (aged 15-29) in particular, road accidents are the single largest cause of mortality, responsible for one in four deaths.

Two-wheeler riders account for the highest number of fatalities from road accidents in India, followed by pedestrians. Nearly half of all deaths in road accidents are of people on two-wheelers.

Proportionate to their populations, Tamil Nadu, Telangana and Chhattisgarh have the highest road accident fatality rates.

In Bihar and West Bengal, more than half of the road accident deaths involved pedestrians. Meanwhile, in Goa, Chhattisgarh and Tamil Nadu, over half of road accident deaths involved two-wheelers.

What are the leading causes for these road accident deaths in India? Read @nileena_suresh's analysis to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/road-accident-deaths/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Death #Accident #Roads #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Imagine being Mr. Siddharth Balachandran. You believed that Stock Exchanges will create value. You invested ~80 crores in BSE in early 2018 at market ...
items.description_html:
Imagine being Mr. Siddharth Balachandran.

You believed that Stock Exchanges will create value. You invested ~80 crores in BSE in early 2018 at market cap of Rs 3600 cr and take 2.5% stake.

Two years later by March 2020, the 80 cr investment is down 65% and worth <30 crores.

You hold on, even add some more over the next year taking it to 3% stake. Then you hold it for next 5 years.

Cut to now - his holding value in BSE is over Rs 3,500 crores and he is still holding!

The stock fell 70% before it gave him a 50 bagger! 87x from Covid lows.

This is not just wealth creation, this is legacy creation!

PS: He also holds 0.35% in NSE worth over Rs 1900 cr. He is the man who truly made money from the stock markets wave in India. Picture in comment.

items.title: Nearly 95% of Indian households now have access to basic drinking water, a rate of progress that has surpassed the world's average. The most recent da...
items.description_html:
Nearly 95% of Indian households now have access to basic drinking water, a rate of progress that has surpassed the world's average.

The most recent data on access to drinking water in India comes from the 79th round of the National Sample Survey (NSS), conducted in 2022-23.

Hand pumps and tube wells are the single largest source of drinking water for rural Indians, while piped water is the biggest source for urban Indians.

While government data shows that a higher number of rural households have been provided tap water connections in their homes, survey data numbers are substantially lower. This could stem from the fact that the survey asks households what their principal source of drinking water is. Principal source is the source from which most of the drinking water was obtained by the household during the last 365 days.

How do people from different regions across India fare in terms of access to drinking water? Read @Rukmini’s piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/access-to-drinking-water/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Water #WASH #WashData #India #DataForIndia

In 1990, India recorded around nearly 10 deaths from malaria for every 100,000 people. By 2019, India's

items.title: Globally, the number of deaths from malaria has remained relatively steady since the 1990s. However, India has made significant progress in reducing i...
items.description_html:
Globally, the number of deaths from malaria has remained relatively steady since the 1990s. However, India has made significant progress in reducing its share of these deaths.

In 1990, India recorded around nearly 10 deaths from malaria for every 100,000 people. By 2019, India's rate fell sharply to less than 1 death per 100,000 people.

Data on deaths from malaria in India comes from the Sample Registration System’s (SRS) Cause of Death Survey. To compare India with other countries globally, we use the Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation’s (IHME) Global Burden of Disease database.

Despite this progress, children are particularly vulnerable to malaria on account of their underdeveloped immune systems. In 2019, nearly 1 in every 50 deaths among children aged 4-14 years in India was attributed to malaria.

How does malaria impact people from different age groups and regions across India? Read @nileena_suresh's analysis to learn more: https://www.dataforindia.com/malaria-in-india/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Malaria #Health #PublicHealth #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Greece is turning its back on coal and replacing it with solar and wind— To tackle climate change, the world must transition away from fossil fuels a...
items.description_html:
Greece is turning its back on coal and replacing it with solar and wind—

To tackle climate change, the world must transition away from fossil fuels and towards low-carbon power sources.

Greece is almost there when it comes to coal. Coal became the dominant source of electricity in the second half of the 20th century, but it has fallen out of favor in the 21st.

The chart shows that just over a decade ago, almost half of the country’s power came from coal. This has now fallen to 6%.

Solar and wind have replaced it; their share has tripled in the last decade; when combined, they’ve become the largest source.

As a result, the country’s CO2 emissions from coal have fallen by nearly 90% from their peak, and national emissions as a whole have halved.

(This Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie and Pablo Rosado.)

items.title: Over time, India’s economy has steadily moved away from its dependence on agriculture. The services sector now accounts for over half of India’s gro...
items.description_html:
Over time, India’s economy has steadily moved away from its dependence on agriculture. The services sector now accounts for over half of India’s gross domestic product (GDP) as of 2024.

To varying extents, most Indian states too reflect this shift. India's richest states are the ones where agriculture now accounts for the smallest share of the state's domestic product.

In states like Kerala and Karnataka, services contribute more than 60% of the state's GDP. Gujarat is unusual among richer states, with the manufacturing sector being a much larger contributor to its economy.

In India’s poorer states, on the other hand, agriculture still plays a big role. Madhya Pradesh’s agricultural sector, for instance, contributes to nearly half of the state’s economy, the highest across India. Punjab is a notable outlier - a rich state with a large contribution of agriculture to its economy.

Read @akwaghmare's piece to learn more about this shift, and the sectors growing in different states across India: https://www.dataforindia.com/state-economies/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Agriculture #Services #Economy #India #DataForIndia

items.title: The simplest ways to understand household incomes in India would be to either use income tax assessment data, or by surveying households on their inco...
items.description_html:
The simplest ways to understand household incomes in India would be to either use income tax assessment data, or by surveying households on their incomes.

However, just 7.5% of adult Indians file income tax returns. Additionally, India's statistics ministry does not directly survey respondents on their incomes, because the informal nature of most work in India, coupled with the seasonality of agricultural incomes, makes accurately estimating incomes challenging.

In such cases, economists use household consumption expenditure as a proxy for household incomes - consumption expenditure plus savings minus debt broadly equals income.

Household spending also offers insight into the living standards of the population - richer people broadly spend more and on different kinds of things than poorer people are able to.

How is consumption expenditure measured in India? And how has the Household Consumption Expenditure Survey (HCES) evolved over time? @akwaghmare unpacks the methodology and its history in this piece: https://www.dataforindia.com/hces-measurement/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#ConsumptionExpenditure #HouseholdSpending #LivingStandards #India #DataForIndia

items.title: One of the most effective ways to understand how Indians live is by looking at how much they spend in a month. This is particularly significant in Ind...
items.description_html:
One of the most effective ways to understand how Indians live is by looking at how much they spend in a month. This is particularly significant in India where the economy is largely informal and significantly agricultural, making income measurement a challenge.

An average Indian’s Monthly Per Capita Consumption Expenditure (MPCE) on everything except investments is a little under ₹5,000 or USD 60, according to the 2023 Household Consumption Expenditure Survey.

However, there is a considerable gap between urban and rural spending. The average person in urban India spends around 70% more every month than the average rural Indian.

Urban expenditure is higher than rural expenditure at every step of the distribution, but the gap is most pronounced among those at the highest consumption level. In the lowest quintile (fifth of the population), the average urban MPCE is 50% higher than the average rural MPCE. In the highest quintile, however, urban MPCE is 90% more than rural MPCE.

What do Indians spend their money on, and how much money do the richest 5% of Indians spend? Read @akwaghmare’s piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/consumption-expenditure/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#ConsumptionExpenditure #HouseholdSpending #LivingStandards #India #DataForIndia

items.title: There has been a significant expansion in access to electricity for Indian households over the last three decades. In 1993, Delhi was the only state/U...
items.description_html:
There has been a significant expansion in access to electricity for Indian households over the last three decades.

In 1993, Delhi was the only state/Union Territory with more than 95% of households connected to electricity. By 2021, 31 of the 36 states and Union Territories had electrified over 95% of their households.

However, having an electricity connection doesn't guarantee reliable service. Households can still face challenges with frequent power cuts, voltage fluctuations, and inconsistent supply. To understand how many Indian households are using electricity regularly, we look at data from the Household Consumption Expenditure Surveys, which ask households about their primary energy source for lighting.

In 1983, more than eight in ten rural households primarily used kerosene to light their homes. That share was down to virtually zero by 2023. Electricity lights most Indian homes now.

Even with all this progress, one in five of the poorest rural households did not have access to electricity as of 2021. To learn more about this disparity in access, and how much electricity most Indians consume, read @nileena_suresh’s piece: https://www.dataforindia.com/access-to-electricity/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Electricity #Power #Lighting #India #DataForIndia

items.title: India represents the world's second-largest labour market, with approximately 625 million workers. Of these, 600 million are currently employed, with ...
items.description_html:
India represents the world's second-largest labour market, with approximately 625 million workers. Of these, 600 million are currently employed, with the rest seeking work. Agriculture employs just under half of these workers or 280 million people.

Outside of agriculture, the vast majority of Indians work in informal enterprises, which are unregistered enterprises run primarily at the household-level. Many developing economies have a high share of informal workers, as opposed to advanced economies.

Additionally, about 10% of Indian non-farm workers are in the public sector. This includes government departments, public sector enterprises, and autonomous organisations funded by the government.

Most of the government jobs are in schools, railways and public sector banks, in government-funded road and other construction projects, and in power and water supply utilities. Rural health workers and panchayat officials also form a part of rural government jobs.

Finally, 11% of non-agricultural jobs are in the private formal sector. Within this small slice, half of India's private sector jobs are in manufacturing, while information technology has the next biggest share.

How is employment distributed across agriculture, industry, and services sectors in India, and what types of jobs are found in each sector? Read @akwaghmare’s piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/work-employment-in-india/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Work #Employment #Job #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Globally, India has one of the lowest number of registered cars, but highest number of two-wheelers per 1,000 people. Indian roads host around 260 mil...
items.description_html:
Globally, India has one of the lowest number of registered cars, but highest number of two-wheelers per 1,000 people.

Indian roads host around 260 million two-wheelers and 50 million cars. Relative to the country’s population, there are 185 two-wheelers and 34 cars for every 1,000 people in India.

However, the number of vehicles by itself doesn't tell the complete story. Some households in India may own multiple vehicles while others may have none. The 2023 Household Consumption Expenditure Survey found that more than half of Indian households owned a two-wheeler, but fewer than one in ten households owned a car.

Additionally, more than 70% of households in India’s richer states like Gujarat and Tamil Nadu have two-wheelers, while household two-wheeler ownership is lower in the relatively poorer eastern states. The richest 20% of Indians are twice as likely to own two-wheelers as those in the poorest 20%.

Read @akwaghmare’s piece to learn more about the vehicle ownership trends across India: https://www.dataforindia.com/vehicle-ownership/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#TwoWheeler #Car #Vehicle #India #DataForIndia

items.title: What do governments spend money on? In the chart, we see spending in different categories, such as defense, health, and education, as a share of total...
items.description_html:
What do governments spend money on?

In the chart, we see spending in different categories, such as defense, health, and education, as a share of total government spending. This is shown for a selection of OECD countries.

For some categories, such as public services, the share spent is fairly similar across countries: it ranges from a low of 9% in Japan to a high of 15% in the US and Finland. Public services includes things like the running of core government functions, foreign aid, and paying interest on government debt.

In other categories, such as social protection, the difference across countries is much larger: from a low of 21% in the US to a high of 44% in Finland. Social protection includes things like pensions, unemployment benefits, disability support, and other benefits.

It’s important to keep in mind that the total amount of government spending varies a lot across countries, so even though the share we see here might be similar, the actual spending might be far different.

items.title: One of the most remarkable stories of change in India has been the successful effort to bring most children into school. However, keeping young people...
items.description_html:
One of the most remarkable stories of change in India has been the successful effort to bring most children into school. However, keeping young people in education beyond school remains a challenge.

To understand how many children in a particular age group are in school, we look at a measure called the enrolment ratio. The Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) is the number of children enrolled in an education level relative to their population.

At the primary level (Grades 1 to 5), India's gross enrolment ratio is more than 100%. This is as a result of some underage or overage children being enrolled in primary classes. Thereafter, the gross enrolment ratio declines at each successive stage of education, as more and more children drop out of school.

While India has surpassed the global average in primary and secondary school enrolment, it is still lower than the world average for higher education.

To learn more about educational pursuits of India’s students and the factors contributing to keeping some of them out of the classroom, read @akwaghmare's piece: https://www.dataforindia.com/enrolment-in-education/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Enrolment #Education #HigherEducation #India #DataForIndia

items.title: China is moving much faster on electric cars than the EU or the United States— Road transport is responsible for around three-quarters of global carb...
items.description_html:
China is moving much faster on electric cars than the EU or the United States—

Road transport is responsible for around three-quarters of global carbon dioxide emissions from transport. Switching from petrol and diesel to electric vehicles is an important solution to decarbonize our economies.

This chart shows the change in share of new cars that were electric in China, the European Union (EU), and the United States (US) between 2020 and 2023. This includes fully electric and plug-in hybrid cars, though most are fully electric.

In 2020, electric cars were rare everywhere. But by 2023, over one-third of new vehicles in China were electric, compared to less than a quarter in the EU and under a tenth in the US.

While we only have annual data up to 2023, preliminary figures suggest that in 2024, electric cars outsold conventional ones for the first time in China.

(This Data Insight was written by @S_VanTeutem.)

items.title: The risk of dying of an infectious disease is closely related to the income levels of the country you live in. In countries with lower income levels, ...
items.description_html:
The risk of dying of an infectious disease is closely related to the income levels of the country you live in. In countries with lower income levels, most deaths are caused by communicable diseases and childbirth-related conditions, as access to nutrition, healthcare and a clean environment are limited.

As countries become more affluent, these causes of death become increasingly preventable and less frequent. Consequently, non-communicable diseases such as heart disease and cancer become the predominant causes of mortality.

India is in the midst of this transition, a moment that in public health is called the epidemiological transition from communicable diseases and conditions surrounding childbirth to non-communicable diseases as the leading cause of mortality.

The share of deaths from communicable diseases in India dropped from nearly half of all deaths in 2000, to below one in four by 2019.

However, the extent of the shift from communicable diseases to non-communicable diseases varies by region within India. To learn more about this transition, read @Rukmini's piece: https://www.dataforindia.com/indias-disease-transition/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Diseases #Health #PublicHealth #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Most Indians are regular consumers of dairy products (milk or curd). Half of adult Indians consume dairy products every day, while three out of four h...
items.description_html:
Most Indians are regular consumers of dairy products (milk or curd). Half of adult Indians consume dairy products every day, while three out of four have them at least once a week. Only one in twenty Indians do not consume any dairy products.

Data on dairy consumption comes from India's National Family Health Surveys, which ask a representative sample of the population about their food practices. India's National Nutritional Guidelines recommend that adults consume three servings of milk and dairy products of 100 ml each every day, or 300 ml of dairy in all every day.

Dairy consumption in India is sharply affected by income, with consumption rising steadily by wealth group. Regular dairy consumption is more common in the richer northern and southern states, and is lowest in the less developed eastern states of Odisha, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand and West Bengal.

Consumption of dairy is higher in urban India, where two out of three adults consume dairy at least weekly, whereas in rural areas, just half do so.

Over time, Indians are consuming more dairy, and spending more of their food budgets on dairy products. Read @nileena_suresh’s piece to learn more: https://www.dataforindia.com/dairy-consumption/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post`

#Dairy #Nutrition #Health #India #DataForIndia

items.title: The self-employed make up the majority of the Indian workforce. India's labour statistics define the self-employed as persons who operate their own fa...
items.description_html:
The self-employed make up the majority of the Indian workforce.

India's labour statistics define the self-employed as persons who operate their own farm or non-farm enterprise, or are engaged independently in a profession or trade on their own, or with one or a few partners in household enterprises.

Unlike salaried and casual workers, the self-employed do not get paid a wage, but their earnings come from the profits of the enterprise.

In India, self-employment is higher in rural areas than in urban areas.

The rural self-employed work largely in agriculture, where they are cultivators, or livestock and dairy producers, or engaged in allied activities like crop-cutting. The urban self-employed, meanwhile, are for the most part in services, where they run or work as salespersons in retail shops, or are wholesale traders, or work as self-employed drivers, among the key occupations.

When it comes to men and women, however, self-employment plays out differently in India. Read @akwaghmare’s piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/self-employment/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Employment #SelfEmployment #Work #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Across the world and over time, women choose to have fewer children when incomes, access to education and healthcare improve. India has seen the same ...
items.description_html:
Across the world and over time, women choose to have fewer children when incomes, access to education and healthcare improve.

India has seen the same trend. The share of mothers with three or more children dropped from over 60% in 1993 to 40% by 2021.

However, while Indian women are having fewer children now, they are not having these children much later in life. Instead, as families become smaller, more women are completing all their pregnancies in their twenties and early thirties.

Over the past two decades, the age at which women have their first child has risen only slightly, by less than two years. The median age at which an Indian mother has her last child, on the other hand, has fallen substantially, by more than five years.

How are the birth trends shifting for Indian women in different age groups? Read @Rukmini’s analysis to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/mother-age-childbirth/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Population #Fertility #TFR #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Access to mobile phones and the internet in India have expanded rapidly over the last decade. However, there is a gap in the technical skills needed t...
items.description_html:
Access to mobile phones and the internet in India have expanded rapidly over the last decade. However, there is a gap in the technical skills needed to use these technologies effectively to communicate, learn and work.

India is a signatory to the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), one of which is to substantially increase the proportion of young people with information and communications technology (ICT) skills by 2030.

The Indian National Statistics Office's 2022-23 Comprehensive Annual Modular Survey (CAMS) collects this data on the prevalence of nine ICT skills listed in the SDGs.

While India’s youth, aged 15-24, are more likely to have ICT skills than many older age groups, they lag behind advanced economies like Japan. ICT skills among young Indians compare well, however, with emerging countries such as Brazil and Bangladesh.

Even within India, there are large ICT skill gaps between rural and urban Indians, and between men and women. Read @akwaghmare's analysis to learn more: https://www.dataforindia.com/ict-skills/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#ICT #Technology #DigitalIndia #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Over half of all women aged 15-49 in India were anaemic as of 2021, according to the National Family Health Survey’s (NFHS) 5th round. While educatio...
items.description_html:
Over half of all women aged 15-49 in India were anaemic as of 2021, according to the National Family Health Survey’s (NFHS) 5th round.

While education and wealth are associated with slightly lower levels of anaemia, prevalence remains high even among the most educated and richest women. Even within the richest 20% of Indians, nearly half of women are anaemic.

Not only are India's anaemia numbers high, but NFHS data also suggests that the prevalence of anaemia in India rose between 2015-16 and 2019-21, the two most recent rounds of the survey.

India's high rates of anaemia are something of a puzzle in global health, as during the same period, other indicators of health, like stunting among children and adults, or the share of children and adults who reported receiving adequate nutrition, improved.

The answer may partly lie in how India measures anaemia.

Data on anaemia in India and many developing countries is collected using the capillary method, compared to the more sophisticated venous method. According to one recent systematic review, the capillary method that India uses produces higher and less reliable estimates.

The Indian government has already begun experimenting with a new survey using the venous method. Read @Rukmini’s piece to find out more: https://www.dataforindia.com/anaemia-in-india/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Women #Children #Anaemia #Health #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Historically, when countries moved away from an agrarian economy, economic growth first came from industry, followed by a much later growth in service...
items.description_html:
Historically, when countries moved away from an agrarian economy, economic growth first came from industry, followed by a much later growth in services. This was true for the early industrialisers of Europe and North America, as well as Japan and some other East Asian economies.

For India though, the move to services came much earlier.

Industry in India is composed chiefly of manufacturing and construction, with mining and utilities being smaller components. The share of industry in the economy grew only slightly from 23% in 1973 to 28% in 2023.

Additionally the share of manufacturing in India's GDP, historically the largest component of industry, declined from 1960 to 2023, having hit its peak in the 1990s. This is substantially different from the trajectory of South Korea or Bangladesh, where manufacturing continues to drive growth.

The limited growth within industry in India did not come from manufacturing, but from construction. Read @akwaghmare’s piece to learn more about India’s unique move away from agriculture: https://www.dataforindia.com/agriculture-shift/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Agriculture #Industry #Manufacturing #Economy #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Measuring the ownership of physical assets--like a TV, refrigerator, or washing machine--often helps understand the economic situation of a household....
items.description_html:
Measuring the ownership of physical assets--like a TV, refrigerator, or washing machine--often helps understand the economic situation of a household. The Household Consumption Expenditure Survey by the National Statistics Office does exactly that, asking Indian households if they possess any among a list of household assets.

The most widely owned of these assets in India is the television. Two-thirds of Indian households owned a television in 2024. This translates to a little more than 200 million Indian households with a TV in their homes.

In the early 1990s, just 10% of Indian households owned a television. TV ownership grew rapidly since, but the pace of growth has fallen in recent years, suggesting that household ownership of TVs is nearing saturation.

States in the north and the south with higher per capita incomes have a considerably higher ownership of TVs. Even among the poorest 20% of urban Indians, over half now own TV.

How does the ownership of other household assets like refrigerators, washing machines or air conditioners look? Read @akwaghmare's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/households-assets/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#TV #Household #Assets #Economy #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Meat, poultry, seafood and eggs are widely consumed in India. Approximately 80% of Indians aged 15-49 consumed some form of animal-sourced protein (ex...
items.description_html:
Meat, poultry, seafood and eggs are widely consumed in India. Approximately 80% of Indians aged 15-49 consumed some form of animal-sourced protein (excluding dairy) as of 2021, a rise from 74% in 2006.

The National Family Health Survey classifies different types of animal proteins into three broad categories - eggs, fish and chicken/meat. Among these, eggs are the most commonly consumed (by 78% of the population), followed by chicken/meat (75%) and fish (72%).

Among meat eaters, one in ten people eat some form of meat daily, while six in ten do so weekly. Both the share of meat consumers and their frequency of consumption are higher in urban areas.

Only four Indian states have a vegetarian majority, and in most coastal states, less than 5% of the population does not eat meat. For more details, read @nileena_suresh's analysis: https://www.dataforindia.com/meat-consumption/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

items.title: Life expectancy - the average age that a child could expect to live to - has been steadily increasing in India. A child born in 1970 could expect to l...
items.description_html:
Life expectancy - the average age that a child could expect to live to - has been steadily increasing in India. A child born in 1970 could expect to live only until her 50th birthday. By 2018, life expectancy had risen to over 70 years.

In the 1970s when comparable data first became available, Indian men lived longer than women. That male advantage was reversed in the early 1980s, and a girl born in the late 2010s could now expect to live nearly three years longer than a boy born in the same year.

However, life expectancy is not uniform across the country - a child born in Delhi can expect to live more than ten years longer than a child born in Chhattisgarh today. In most states, there is also a significant urban advantage, and an urban Indian can expect to live more than 4.5 years longer than a child born in rural India.

What are the factors driving this increase in Indian life expectancy? Read @Rukmini's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/population-mortality/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Population #Mortality #LifeExpectancy #India #DataForIndia

items.title: India has the world's largest emigrant population, with over 18.5 million people of Indian origin living overseas in 2024, accounting for 6% of all in...
items.description_html:
India has the world's largest emigrant population, with over 18.5 million people of Indian origin living overseas in 2024, accounting for 6% of all international migrants globally.

According to the United Nations, an international migrant is someone who has moved across an international border and changed their usual place of residence, typically living in the new country continuously for at least 12 months.

The international migrant stock, as measured by the UN, refers to the total number of international migrants in a country at a given time. Between 1990 and 2024, the migrant stock of Indian origin nearly tripled from 6.5 million to 18.5 million.

Indian emigration remains concentrated within Asia, particularly in West Asia, where half of all Indian emigrants live. One-quarter of Indian out-migrants live in North America, a share that has grown over time, as has emigration to Australia and New Zealand.

Meanwhile, although more Indians have moved to Europe in absolute numbers, its share in total Indian emigrants has declined. To learn more about the international migratory trends of Indians, read @nileena_suresh’s analysis: https://www.dataforindia.com/international-migration/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Migration #Immigration #Emigration #India #DataForIndia

items.title: China reduced extreme poverty rapidly, but Indonesia hasn't been far behind— China is often the poster child for rapid reductions in poverty, and for...
items.description_html:
China reduced extreme poverty rapidly, but Indonesia hasn't been far behind—

China is often the poster child for rapid reductions in poverty, and for good reason: in the early 1980s, over 90% of its population lived in extreme poverty, but by the early 2020s, that number had dropped to nearly zero.

Some people assume that China is the only reason global extreme poverty has declined. But that’s wrong: many other countries have seen dramatic reductions in poverty. Indonesia is one clear example; it’s shown alongside China on the chart.

In 1984, three-quarters of Indonesians lived on less than $2.15 per day. By 2023, this had fallen to less than 2%. While it didn’t quite match China’s decline, it has still been impressive. The number of people living in extreme poverty has fallen from 120 million to 5 million.

Note that the international poverty line is extremely low, defined as people living on less than $2.15 per day. But Indonesia has also made progress measured by higher poverty lines.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie.)

items.title: India's economy is now the fifth largest in the world, and projected to become the third largest by 2027. The size of a country's economy is measured ...
items.description_html:
India's economy is now the fifth largest in the world, and projected to become the third largest by 2027.

The size of a country's economy is measured in terms of the gross domestic product (GDP). The GDP is the sum of the monetary value of everything that is produced in the country, or in other words, everything that is consumed, invested in and exported out of the country.

India's GDP in 2023 was ₹300 trillion (₹300 lakh crore). In dollar terms, it was $3.7 trillion.

While the GDP reflects the total size of a country's economy, it does not on its own give a sense of individual well-being. As a result, looking at the country's GDP relative to its population produces a better picture of what well-being in the country looks like.

The per capita income is the GDP divided by population. India's per capita income was nearly ₹186,000 in 2023. In dollar terms, India's per capita income in 2022 was nearly $2,400 per year, according to the World Bank.

This means that while India was fifth among 200 countries in terms of total GDP, it was 158th out of 200 countries in terms of GDP per capita.

Is per capita income the best way to understand people's well-being? Read @akwaghmare’s piece to learn more about India’s economy: https://www.dataforindia.com/gdp/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Economy #PerCapitaIncome #GDP #India #DataForIndia

items.title: With over 1.3 billion people, India is the most populous country in the world. But since the 1990s, India's population growth has been gradually slowi...
items.description_html:
With over 1.3 billion people, India is the most populous country in the world. But since the 1990s, India's population growth has been gradually slowing down.

One reason behind this slowdown is the declining Total Fertility Rate (TFR), the average number of children a woman is likely to have in her lifetime. As a result, the absolute number of children born every year in India began to decline from the early 2000s.

Beyond TFR, a country's population growth is also affected by the number of people who die every year. Until now, India's population growth slowdown was powered by falling fertility alone. But rising mortality has begun to play a role too. As India grows older, mortality rates are beginning to rise.

Despite the slowdown, by the mid-2060s, India is projected to be at the peak of its size, with nearly 1.7 billion people. India is also expected to continue to be the world's most populous country until beyond 2100 which is the furthest year out for which population projections have been made.

Read @Rukmini's piece to learn more about the population trends of India: https://www.dataforindia.com/population-growth/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Population #Census #PopulationData #India #DataForIndia

items.title: The most widely used indicator to understand how risky pregnancy and childbirth in a region can be is the maternal mortality ratio (MMR), or the numbe...
items.description_html:
The most widely used indicator to understand how risky pregnancy and childbirth in a region can be is the maternal mortality ratio (MMR), or the number of maternal deaths for every 100,000 live births. Maternal deaths are deaths during pregnancy or within 42 days after, attributed to causes related to or aggravated by the pregnancy, excluding accidental or unrelated incidents.

In 2020, 97 women died of maternal causes for every 100,000 live births in India, translating to around 24,000 maternal deaths in one year alone. India’s maternal mortality data comes from the Sample Registration System (SRS). The most recent SRS report on maternal mortality, covers the period from 2018 to 2020.

While India has witnessed a steady decline in maternal mortality, regional disparities continue to exist. India is also still some distance away from meeting international commitments on reducing maternal mortality. Read @nileena_suresh's piece to learn more: https://www.dataforindia.com/maternal-mortality/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Mortality #Mother #Death #Health #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Most Indian adults have low levels of education, with just over one in ten having completed a higher education programme. This data comes from the 202...
items.description_html:
Most Indian adults have low levels of education, with just over one in ten having completed a higher education programme. This data comes from the 2022-23 Periodic Labour Force Survey.

Looking at the population by age groups, however, shows that the level of educational attainment of Indians has improved substantially over time. While more than half of those who are in their sixties or older are illiterate, illiteracy rates among those now in their twenties are in the single digits.

Even though the share of the population that is illiterate has declined sharply over time, few Indians go on to get a higher education. Just over two in ten Indians even in the age-group of 25-29 have attained higher education.

Read @akwaghmare's piece to learn more about the education levels across various socio-economic groups in India: https://www.dataforindia.com/education-levels-in-india/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Education #Literacy #HigherEducation #School #College #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Global cereal production has grown much faster than population in the last half-century— The world's population has more than doubled over the last 6...
items.description_html:
Global cereal production has grown much faster than population in the last half-century—

The world's population has more than doubled over the last 60 years, but global food production has managed to keep up.

The chart shows the change in four indicators since 1961: cereal production, cereal yields, land use for cereals, and population. Each metric is expressed in its relative change since 1961 (which is given a value of zero).

Cereal production has increased by 3.5-fold, more than the 2.6-fold growth in population. That means production has grown faster than the population, and the world produces more cereal per person than 60 years ago.

We can also see what has driven this increase. You can grow more food by either increasing crop yields or using more land. While land use has increased, most of this growth has come from higher crop yields. This has spared some natural habitats from being converted into farmland.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie.)

items.title: Only four in ten Indian women participate in the labour force (people who are either working or looking for work as a share of the population), accord...
items.description_html:
Only four in ten Indian women participate in the labour force (people who are either working or looking for work as a share of the population), according to India's most recent labour statistics.

This number is lower than in many countries at comparable levels of income and development. But many of the 300 million Indian women out of the labour force are working, just not in the way that labour statistics define work.

How is work captured in Indian statistics? What do women in the paid workforce do? Read @akwaghmare's piece to find out more: https://www.dataforindia.com/women-and-work/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Work #Women #Employment #LFPR #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Over the last two decades, India has seen a big push towards financial inclusion. Having a bank account is an important first step in that journey, al...
items.description_html:
Over the last two decades, India has seen a big push towards financial inclusion. Having a bank account is an important first step in that journey, allowing account-holders to receive welfare benefits, save, borrow and invest.

From fewer than half of Indian households having a bank account in 2006, 96% of households had a bank account by 2021, according to the National Family Health Survey.

Even at the individual level, over nine in ten adult Indians now report having a bank account. Access to a bank account is high across income groups; even among the poorest 20% of Indians, nine in ten now have a bank account.

But despite most adults now having a bank account, the extent of usage could still have some way to go. Read @akwaghmare's piece to find out more: https://www.dataforindia.com/access-to-banking/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Banking #Bank #Economy #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Which countries have contributed the most to historical CO₂ emissions? When we emit carbon dioxide (CO₂) into the atmosphere, most of it stays there...
items.description_html:
Which countries have contributed the most to historical CO₂ emissions?

When we emit carbon dioxide (CO₂) into the atmosphere, most of it stays there for centuries or millennia. This means that CO₂ emitted even a century ago has contributed to the rising temperatures we see today.

In other words, how much the climate warms depends on how much cumulative CO₂ is emitted over time.

The chart shows the ten countries with the largest share of the world’s historical emissions, based on cumulative emissions from fossil fuels and industry since 1750.

The United States has contributed the most, accounting for almost one quarter. This is followed by China and Russia.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie.)

items.title: India's female labour force participation rate (LFPR) - the share of women who are either working or seeking work - is low by global standards. Histor...
items.description_html:
India's female labour force participation rate (LFPR) - the share of women who are either working or seeking work - is low by global standards. Historically, a majority of adult Indian women reported that they were attending to household duties and were not available for paid work. However, recent trends show an increase in female LFPR.

Currently, there are roughly 215 million women in the Indian labour force, of which 7 million women are unemployed. But the nature of work among women workers is materially different across India.

India's labour statistics classify workers depending on their working arrangements into four categories - salaried, casual workers, self-employed, and unpaid helpers.

Recent trends show that rural women are increasingly becoming self-employed and earning their own wages. Meanwhile, urban women are increasingly taking up salaried jobs. As a result, the share of casual employment has substantially decreased across both rural and urban India.

Additionally, more than one in three working women, nearly 70 million women, are involved in work as unpaid helpers. They are doing economically productive work, but for no wages. Read @akwaghmare's piece to further decode the female LFPR trends in India: https://www.dataforindia.com/female-lfpr-change/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Work #Women #Employment #LFPR #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Under the United Nations' Sustainable Development Goal 6.2, India has committed to eliminating open defecation and providing equitable access to sanit...
items.description_html:
Under the United Nations' Sustainable Development Goal 6.2, India has committed to eliminating open defecation and providing equitable access to sanitation and hygiene for all by 2030.

Administrative data from the Indian government captures the number of toilets built. However, to understand actual usage rather than just access, we look at data on toilet usage from the 2021 National Family Health Survey.

One in five Indian households still do not use a toilet facility, according to the survey data. There is also a significant difference between the usage of toilet facilities in rural and urban areas. One in four rural households did not use a toilet facility as of 2021; in contrast, only 6% reported the same in urban households.

The primary reason why people do not use toilets is a lack of access. However, about one in ten households report not using toilet facilities despite having access to them—a pattern particularly notable in urban areas.

Over half of the Indian households that are forced to defecate in the open reside in just four states - Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Maharashtra and Tamil Nadu. Read @nileena_suresh's piece to find out more: https://www.dataforindia.com/sanitation/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Toilets #WASHData #WASH #India #DataForIndia

items.title: It's amusing to see GoI suddenly realising the merit of low tariffs after being pressed by a person who loves tariffs. High tariffs and other barriers...
items.description_html:
It's amusing to see GoI suddenly realising the merit of low tariffs after being pressed by a person who loves tariffs.

High tariffs and other barriers have hurt our competitiveness and we should have got rid of them much earlier.

The political expediency masks a step that's economically prudent.

But then that's exactly what Robert Putnam's "Diplomacy and Domestic Politics" predicted. Politicians use international political constraints to overcome domestic political constraints and vice versa.

That's what GoI could pull off in 1991. Can we repeat it now?

items.title: India exports goods and services worth $780 billion, while its imports are worth $850 billion. The total value of India's imports has consistently exc...
items.description_html:
India exports goods and services worth $780 billion, while its imports are worth $850 billion. The total value of India's imports has consistently exceeded its exports in recent decades.

A recurring pattern in India's trade, like in many other emerging economies, involves importing raw materials and intermediate goods, and then exporting finished products. For example, India imports crude oil which is processed into end products like diesel and airline fuel that are exported. Similarly, India both domestically manufactures and imports components for smartphones—such as batteries and displays—and exports the finished devices.

Over the last few years, India’s growth in exports of services has outstripped growth in the export of goods, driven largely by the expanding software services export market. Both software and business services exports have increased nearly 15-fold over the past two decades.

To learn more about how India's exports and imports shape its trade relationships globally, read @akwaghmare's piece: https://www.dataforindia.com/trade/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Imports #Exports #Goods #Services #Trade #Economy #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Based on our understanding of the CDMO Industry Generic CDMOs: Alivus Lifesciences, Windlas, Senores, Akums drugs, Innova Captab. These are those CDMO...
items.description_html:
Based on our understanding of the CDMO Industry

Generic CDMOs: Alivus Lifesciences, Windlas, Senores, Akums drugs, Innova Captab. These are those CDMOs which are doing contract manufacturing for drugs which are off patent.

Mix of Generics+Patented CDMOs: Hikal, Aarti Pharma Labs, Jubilant Pharmova, Laurus (doing more innovator work now). These are those CDMOs which do contract manufacturing for both Patented and off patent drugs in their CDMO verticals.

Patented CDMO businesses: Suven Pharma, Neuland Labs, Blue Jet, Divis Labs, Ami organics. These are those businesses which are doing manufacturing for Patented medicines in their CDMO verticals.

Contract research organisations:

Syngene
Sai life sciences (also have a CDMO business)

These organisations do research/development work for innovators.

Contract testing organisation: VIMTA Labs

These organisations do bio-equivalance testing for different generic pharma companies before they launch a new drug.

Disclaimer: no recommendation to buy or sell. We teach about different industries and how to analyse businesses at https://learn.soic.in

items.title: Less than 40 million young Indians (age 18-23) were enroled in higher education programmes as of 2022. This includes both undergraduate (or equivalent...
items.description_html:
Less than 40 million young Indians (age 18-23) were enroled in higher education programmes as of 2022. This includes both undergraduate (or equivalent) and postgraduate (or equivalent) levels of study.

Of the 33 million enroled in undergraduate programmes, the Arts discipline has the highest inflow of students, followed by Engineering. A notable gender gap exists at the undergraduate level, with male students outnumbering females, particularly in Engineering where the ratio is more than two to one.

India's higher education enrolment rate stands at 32%, which is below the global average of 42%. In China, for comparison, over 70% of young adults are enroled in higher education.

Read @akwaghmare 's piece to learn more about India’s higher education trends, including postgraduate composition and regional nuances that persist: https://www.dataforindia.com/higher-education/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

items.title: Half of India’s population is under the age of 30, making it a far younger country than its European, North American or East Asian counterparts. The ...
items.description_html:
Half of India’s population is under the age of 30, making it a far younger country than its European, North American or East Asian counterparts. The median American or Chinese person, for instance, is over a decade older than the median Indian.

However, India is ageing. From the early 1970s, the share of children (aged 0-14) in the population began to decline, and from the 1980s, the share of young adults (aged 14-29) in the population began to fall. By the mid 2030s, the median Indian will be older than the world’s median.

This is a result of a combination of two key factors: declining fertility rates resulting in fewer children, and people living longer as the country’s health outcomes improve.

Within India though, there are wide variations between states in the ages of their populations. Read @Rukmini's piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/age-distribution-states/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Population #Census #Age #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Weather forecasts have become much more accurate. A four-day forecast today is as accurate as a one-day forecast 30 years ago. The chart here shows th...
items.description_html:
Weather forecasts have become much more accurate. A four-day forecast today is as accurate as a one-day forecast 30 years ago.

The chart here shows the difference between the forecast and the actual weather outcome for forecasts 3, 5, 7, and 10 days in advance. The metric used here is the “500 hPa geopotential height”, a commonly used meteorological measure of air pressure — which dictates weather patterns.

The solid line is for the Northern Hemisphere, and the dashed line is for the Southern.

Three-day forecasts have been pretty accurate since the 1980s, and have still gotten a lot better over time. Today the accuracy is around 97%.

The biggest improvements we’ve seen are for longer timeframes. By the early 2000s, 5-day forecasts were “highly accurate” and 7-day forecasts are reaching that threshold today. 10-day forecasts aren’t quite there yet but are getting better.

This data comes from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF), which produces global numerical weather models and publishes analyses of its errors over time. While national weather agencies use much higher-resolution processing to get local forecasts, these global models provide a crucial input into those more local systems.

items.title: With nearly 160,000 deaths in 2019, road accidents are a major public health concern in India. India reports the highest number of road accident death...
items.description_html:
With nearly 160,000 deaths in 2019, road accidents are a major public health concern in India.

India reports the highest number of road accident deaths in the world, and accounts for 11% of the world's road accidents. Even then, India's mortality rate from road accidents is lower than the global average.

However over the last five decades, India’s road accident fatalities relative to population has been steadily rising, now exceeding those of neighbouring countries like Pakistan and Bangladesh.

The impact is particularly severe among young men aged 15-29, where road accidents are responsible for one in four deaths. Two-wheeler riders face the highest risk, comprising nearly half of all road accident victims, with pedestrians being the second most vulnerable group.

Read @nileena_suresh's piece to learn more about the key causes of these road accident deaths in India: https://www.dataforindia.com/road-accident-deaths/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Death #Accident #Roads #India #DataForIndia

items.title: India isn’t competing with China—it’s competing with 200+ Chinese cities. Analysing India-China competition from a national level is not helpful. W...
items.description_html:
India isn’t competing with China—it’s competing with 200+ Chinese cities.

Analysing India-China competition from a national level is not helpful.

We need competitiveness at the city level. China is not federal and yet more decentralised than India. While India's city-level governments account for less than three per cent of total government spending, a staggering 51 per cent of government spending in China happens at sub-provincial levels.

Local governments also have a much broader qualitative mandate. They are almost exclusively responsible for unemployment insurance and pensions.

This factor alone has enabled many experiments—good and bad—to happen rapidly.

While the industrial successes we often hear about are attributed to China’s top leadership, they are often the result of local governments’ fail-fast approach, investments, corruption, greed and commitment.

items.title: In 2009, only 2% of Indian homes had internet access. With the introduction of mobile broadband, this grew to 30% by 2014 and about 80% by 2023. Along...
items.description_html:
In 2009, only 2% of Indian homes had internet access. With the introduction of mobile broadband, this grew to 30% by 2014 and about 80% by 2023.

Alongside access, the usage of the internet in India has risen too. Six in ten Indians had used the internet at least once in the last three months, as of 2023. This puts India at roughly 100th out of 160 countries in terms of internet use.

But internet usage in India still skews towards younger, more urban, better educated, richer Indians who are more likely to be male. Read @akwaghmare’s piece (link in bio) to learn more about the progress of phones and internet in India, and how Indians use them: https://www.dataforindia.com/comm-tech/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Phone #Internet #Connectivity #India #DataForIndia

items.title: In the early 2000s, fewer than eight in ten Indians had access to basic drinking water, lower than the world average. Over the last two decades, India...
items.description_html:
In the early 2000s, fewer than eight in ten Indians had access to basic drinking water, lower than the world average. Over the last two decades, India has made steady progress. Nearly 95% of Indian households now have access to basic drinking water, outpacing global progress.

This increased access has also come with a steady rise in the share of Indians whose principal source of drinking water is bottled water. So much so that 15% of urban Indians now get their drinking water from bottled water.

Bottled water, as defined by India's National Statistics Office, covers "drinking water packaged in bottles, jars, pouches, and similar containers". This also includes the ubiquitous bubble top containers which are such an Indian urban staple now.

The southern Indian states in particular are beginning to see a heavy reliance on bottled water. Over a third of urban households, and nearly half of rural households in Telangana now rely on bottled water.

Read @Rukmini's piece to find out more: https://www.dataforindia.com/access-to-drinking-water/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Water #WASH #WashData #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Global sales of combustion engine cars have peaked— To decarbonize road transport, the world must move away from petrol (gasoline) and diesel cars an...
items.description_html:
Global sales of combustion engine cars have peaked—

To decarbonize road transport, the world must move away from petrol (gasoline) and diesel cars and towards electric vehicles and other forms of low-carbon transport.

This transition has already started. In fact, global sales of combustion engine cars are well past the peak and are now falling.

As you can see in the chart, global sales peaked in 2018. This is calculated based on data from the International Energy Agency. Bloomberg New Energy Finance estimates this peak occurred one year earlier, in 2017.

Sales of electric cars, on the other hand, are growing quickly.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie.)
