items.title: Literacy - the ability to read and write a sentence in any language with proper comprehension - is the most fundamental measure of educational outcome...
items.description_html:
Literacy - the ability to read and write a sentence in any language with proper comprehension - is the most fundamental measure of educational outcomes.

India has made remarkable progress in improving literacy rates, rising from fewer than two in ten literate Indians in the early 1950s, to eight in ten as of 2023. This data comes from the Census (until 2011) and subsequent nationally representative sample surveys by the National Statistics Office (NSO).

While literacy in India lags behind the world average, data shows that that is largely a historical legacy. Youth literacy is rising fast, suggesting that India is on the path to near-universal literacy.

However, significant disparities persist across demographic groups. Male literacy rates exceed female literacy rates, and urban areas show higher literacy levels than rural regions.

Additionally, literacy rates are substantially lower among marginalised groups including Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes. Read @akwaghmare's piece to learn more: https://www.dataforindia.com/literacy-in-india/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Literacy #Education #LivingConditions #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Above is an analysis of large, mid and small caps by business buckets which generally have vastly different PE multiples because of their inherent nat...
items.description_html:
Above is an analysis of large, mid and small caps by business buckets which generally have vastly different PE multiples because of their inherent nature. PSUs and banks trade at relatively low PE multiples and hence depending on their size in a universe, skews the weighted PEx. E.g. in large caps, PSU contribute to 33% of the total profit pool while in small caps it's contribution is only 9%. Hence looking at the total PEx is Apples vs Oranges comparison.

Weighted PEx also can be mis leading because just handful of companies (w/ huge profits or losses) can pull up or down the PEx for that index/universe. As an investor your allocation to a stock in a portfolio isn't proportional to the weight of profit or market cap. Often median PEx provides better picture of the valuation.

As you can see that Weighted PEx of large cap universe is 21.6x vs 29.9x for the small cap universe. This is because of huge contribution by the PSU and Pvt Banks (in large caps) who trade at relatively low multiples. Hence comparing median valuation of the individual buckets is much better comparison. Median PEx for Non PSU or Pvt Banks stocks in the large cap universe is 42x while that in small cap universe is 33.3x.

This analysis is more from a directional perspective rather than exact as the nature of businesses in each buckets would also be different. But blindly accepting comparable multiples thrown at us can also be very damaging to our decision making.

items.title: Almost all livestock in the United States is factory-farmed— It’s difficult to quantify animal suffering in a single number, but one metric often us...
items.description_html:
Almost all livestock in the United States is factory-farmed—

It’s difficult to quantify animal suffering in a single number, but one metric often used to measure living conditions is the number of animals raised on “factory farms”.

Factory farms are defined as “concentrated animal feeding operations” where many animals are held in an intensive feeding operation for more than 45 days.

99% of livestock in the United States is factory-farmed. This latest estimate comes from the Sentience Institute and is based on definitions and data from the US Environment Protection Agency.

The chart shows the share of different forms of livestock that are factory-farmed. More than 98% of chickens, turkeys, hens, pigs, and farmed fish are factory-farmed. Cows are a bit less likely to be factory-farmed, but three-quarters still spend much of their lives in these conditions.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie.)

items.description_html:
Accurately estimating the true burden of malaria in India is challenging. Direct estimates are often under-reported or stunted by accessibility to remote regions. In such cases, modelled estimates are often relied upon to understand the true scale of a disease.

In 2019, the Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation (IHME) models estimated nearly 5.5 million cases of malaria in India, 16x the official estimates by the National Centre for Vector Borne Diseases Control (NCVBDC).

How do we best understand the true scale of malaria cases and deaths in India? Read @nileena_suresh's explainer to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/measuring-malaria/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

items.title: Over the last 30 years, India has seen a sharp decline in the incidence of malaria, sharper than in most of the world. In 1990, India recorded around ...
items.description_html:
Over the last 30 years, India has seen a sharp decline in the incidence of malaria, sharper than in most of the world.

In 1990, India recorded around 4,000 new cases for every 100,000 people, mirroring the global malaria incidence rate. By 2019, while the global incidence rate decreased to 3,000 new cases for every 100,000 people, India's rate fell sharply to less than 400 per 100,000 people.

These figures are based on modelled estimates for disease incidence from the Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation, as directly reported cases of malaria from the Indian government remain limited.

Even with this progress, malaria was still responsible for an estimated 36,000 deaths across India in 2019. Read @nileena_suresh’s piece to understand how malaria impacts different age groups and regions in the country: https://www.dataforindia.com/malaria-in-india/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Malaria #Health #PublicHealth #India #DataForIndia

items.title: One of the most helpful ways to understand how Indians live is by looking at how much they spend in a month. This is particularly significant in India...
items.description_html:
One of the most helpful ways to understand how Indians live is by looking at how much they spend in a month. This is particularly significant in India where the economy is largely informal and agricultural, making income measurement a challenge.

An average Indian’s Monthly Per Capita Consumption Expenditure (MPCE) on everything except investments is a little under ₹5,000 or USD 60, according to the most recent Household Consumption Expenditure Survey. However, there is a considerable gap between urban and rural spending. The average person in rural India spends a little less than ₹4,000 in a month, while an urban Indian spends around Rs 6,500 on average, or 70% more.

Nearly half of rural per-person spending is on food, as against less than 40% in urban areas. Within non-food expenditure, conveyance and purchase of durable goods occupy the biggest share in MPCE.

To learn more about how Indians spend across different food and non-food categories, read @akwaghmare's piece: https://www.dataforindia.com/consumption-expenditure/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#ConsumptionExpenditure #HouseholdSpending #LivingStandards #India #DataForIndia

items.title: India's economic output is concentrated in the southern and western states: they are home to a third of India's population but contribute to more than...
items.description_html:
India's economic output is concentrated in the southern and western states: they are home to a third of India's population but contribute to more than half of the economic output.

Over the last four decades, southern and eastern states have seen a considerable shift in their contribution to the national economy, despite both regions having comparable populations. Southern states like Karnataka are contributing relatively more, while eastern states like West Bengal have seen their share decline.

This divergence becomes even sharper in terms of per capita income, as the richer states are also the ones where population growth has slowed much more dramatically.

In 1981, the per capita income of West Bengal was 16% higher than that of Karnataka. By 2021, Karnataka's relative per capita income was more than double that of West Bengal, showing a reversal of positions, and the widening gap between one of India’s richest and poorest states.

Read @akwaghmare's piece to learn how other states are growing, and how the rate of growth varies significantly between regions: https://www.dataforindia.com/state-economies/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Economy #States #GDP #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Access to electricity in Indian households has expanded dramatically over the last three decades. In 1993, just half of all Indian households had acce...
items.description_html:
Access to electricity in Indian households has expanded dramatically over the last three decades. In 1993, just half of all Indian households had access to electricity, but by 2021, this figure shot up to over 96%.

In 1993, Delhi was the only state/Union Territory with more than 95% of households connected. By 2021, 31 of the 36 states and Union Territories had electrified over 95% of their households.

However, having an electricity connection doesn't guarantee reliable service. Households can still face challenges with frequent power cuts, voltage fluctuations, and inconsistent supply, which affect their ability to use electricity dependably for daily and essential needs.

Read @nileena_suresh's piece to understand how the primary source of energy for household lighting has transformed over time: https://www.dataforindia.com/access-to-electricity/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Electricity #Power #Lighting #India #DataForIndia

items.title: The world has probably passed “peak air pollution”— Global emissions of local air pollutants have probably passed their peak. The chart shows estim...
items.description_html:
The world has probably passed “peak air pollution”—

Global emissions of local air pollutants have probably passed their peak.

The chart shows estimates of global emissions of pollutants such as sulphur dioxide (which causes acid rain), nitrogen oxides, and black and organic carbon.

These pollutants are harmful to human health and can also damage ecosystems.

It looks like emissions have peaked for almost all of these pollutants. Global air pollution is now falling, and we can save many lives by accelerating this decline.

The exception is ammonia, which is mainly produced by agriculture. Its emissions are still rising.

These estimates come from the Community Emissions Data System (CEDS).

(This Daily Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie.)

items.title: Of India's 625 million-strong labour force, approximately 600 million are employed, while 25 million are seeking work. But even among those who are wo...
items.description_html:
Of India's 625 million-strong labour force, approximately 600 million are employed, while 25 million are seeking work. But even among those who are working, the nature/type of employment varies significantly.

Salaried workers, who receive regular monthly payments, comprise about 20% of the workforce (130 million people). In urban areas, salaried jobs make up half of all employment, primarily in sectors like manufacturing, education, health, trade, and technology.

But the meaning of a salaried job is not universal, and all salaried jobs do not have similar benefits. Only 42% have written contracts, 52% receive paid leave, and 46% get at least one social security benefit (gratuity, provident fund, or healthcare).

India’s share of salaried jobs in India is also low compared to 55% in China, 58% in Sri Lanka, and more than 90% in the US. This share has remained unchanged for more than a decade.

The majority of Indian workers—three in five—are self-employed. In rural areas, self-employment predominantly means agricultural work, while urban self-employed workers include small shop owners, food vendors, tailors, and drivers.

@akwaghmare decodes the meaning of work and employment in India, read his piece to learn more: https://www.dataforindia.com/work-employment-in-india/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Work #Employment #Job #India #DataForIndia

items.title: India's roads host nearly 260 million two-wheelers and 50 million cars. This translates to 185 two-wheelers and 34 cars per 1,000 people. However, the...
items.description_html:
India's roads host nearly 260 million two-wheelers and 50 million cars. This translates to 185 two-wheelers and 34 cars per 1,000 people. However, these numbers don't tell the complete story, as some households may own multiple vehicles while others have none.

The recent Household Consumption Expenditure Survey (HCES) provides a more accurate picture of vehicle ownership at the family level. More than half of Indian households own a motorcycle or scooter, while less than one in ten own a car or jeep, according to the 2023 survey.

Two-wheeler ownership shows a relatively broad distribution across economic classes, although households in the richest quintile are twice as likely to own them compared to the poorest quintile. Car ownership, however, follows a more concentrated pattern. In rural areas, car ownership remains low even among the wealthiest quintile. In contrast, 25% of the richest urban households own a car.

Read @akwaghmare's piece to learn more about the transforming landscape of vehicle ownership in India: https://www.dataforindia.com/vehicle-ownership/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#TwoWheeleer #Car #Vehicle #India #DataForIndia

items.title: India is in the midst of a major public health transition, from communicable diseases and childbirth-related conditions to non-communicable diseases a...
items.description_html:
India is in the midst of a major public health transition, from communicable diseases and childbirth-related conditions to non-communicable diseases as the primary cause of death. However, this transition isn't uniform across the country.

Income levels are closely related to diseases that can cause mortality. For groups with lower income levels, most deaths are caused by communicable diseases and childbirth-related conditions, as access to nutrition, healthcare and clean environment are limited. As communities become more affluent and their populations age, these causes of death become increasingly preventable and less frequent. Consequently, non-communicable diseases such as heart disease and cancer become the predominant causes of mortality.

In India's poorest states, communicable diseases still account for nearly 30% of all deaths, while in more developed states, non-communicable diseases are responsible for over half of all mortality.

To learn more about this transition, and use our data explorer to look at all causes of deaths by various age groups and gender, read @Rukmini's piece: https://www.dataforindia.com/indias-disease-transition/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Diseases #Injuries #PublicHealth #India #DataForIndia

items.title: In the last 30 years, almost everybody in Bangladesh gained access to basic electricity— In 1991, around 14% of people in Bangladesh had access to el...
items.description_html:
In the last 30 years, almost everybody in Bangladesh gained access to basic electricity—

In 1991, around 14% of people in Bangladesh had access to electricity. Thirty years later, access was almost universal.

Over 100 million Bangladeshis have gained access to electricity during this time. This enables them to light their homes, use household appliances, or stay connected through phones and the Internet.

The statistic measures the lowest “tier” of energy access: the capacity for basic lighting and charging a phone for at least four hours a day.

But more than half the people in Bangladesh now also have a higher tier of electricity access, which means capacity to power high-load appliances (such as fridges) and electricity for more than eight hours a day.

The UN has set a target to achieve universal access to electricity by 2030. Currently, about 9 in 10 people worldwide have basic access to electricity.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by @antea04.)

items.title: One of the most remarkable stories of change in India has been the successful effort to bring most children into school. Over time, the Gross Enrolmen...
items.description_html:
One of the most remarkable stories of change in India has been the successful effort to bring most children into school. Over time, the Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in India has risen across all levels, most substantially in elementary education.

Historically, most students and young people enrolled in government-run institutions. While enrolment in government schools has declined at the primary level, it rose at the secondary level, and public institutions now account for nearly two in three enrolments across both primary and secondary levels. For higher education, public institutions accounted for half of all enrolments by 2020.

To learn more about educational pursuits of India’s students and the factors contributing to keeping some of them out of school, read @akwaghmare's piece: https://www.dataforindia.com/enrolment-in-education/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Enrolment #Education #PrivateEducation #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Total Fertility Rate (TFR) is a demographic indicator that estimates the average number of children that a woman will have in her lifetime. When a cou...
items.description_html:
Total Fertility Rate (TFR) is a demographic indicator that estimates the average number of children that a woman will have in her lifetime. When a country's TFR drops to 2.1, meaning that a woman is expected to have 2.1 children on average over her lifetime, demographers say that the country has reached 'replacement fertility'.

This is a key milestone in a country's journey, and it indicates that the population will stop growing larger after some time, and only replace itself. If fertility falls further below that level, the population will over time begin to decline in absolute numbers. According to the National Family Health Survey's 5th round (2019-21), India’s TFR has dropped to 2.0 children per woman on average, with some variation between rural and urban areas, and between states.

As a result of this falling fertility, large families have become increasingly rare in India. The share of mothers with four or more children has dropped from over four in ten in 1993 to fewer than one in ten in 2021. This proportion is higher among poorer women and women with lower levels of education.

But although Indian women are having fewer children now, they still have them at a relatively young age. To learn more read @Rukmini's analysis: https://www.dataforindia.com/mother-age-childbirth/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Population #Fertility #TFR #India #DataForIndia

items.title: The world has passed “peak child”— The number of children in the world has stopped growing. This moment in time was given the term “peak child” b...
items.description_html:
The world has passed “peak child”—

The number of children in the world has stopped growing. This moment in time was given the term “peak child” by the late Hans Rosling.

The chart shows the estimated number of children under five years old globally up to 2023, with projections from the United Nations out to 2100. The UN thinks the number of under-5s peaked in 2017.

The chart also shows the number of young people under 15, which peaked in 2021. And the number of under-25s, which may have peaked last year.

“Peak child” is a sign that the world is on course for “peak population”. The UN expects the world population to start falling before the end of the century.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie.)

items.title: Nearly four in five Indians in their 20s can send messages, including via WhatsApp. In comparison, just under half can send emails. At the other end o...
items.description_html:
Nearly four in five Indians in their 20s can send messages, including via WhatsApp. In comparison, just under half can send emails. At the other end of the spectrum, just one in five Indians in their 20s can use spreadsheets, with even fewer able to create presentations or write code.

A significant rural-urban gap exists in these ICT skills, which widens with the relative difficulty level of the skill. There is also a substantial gender gap, with men twice as likely as women to have most ICT skills.

The nature of ICT skills Indians are more comfortable with may be partly due to the widespread adoption of mobile phones versus the slow uptake of computers - more than eight in ten Indian adults can use mobile phones, but only two in ten can use computers. Read @akwaghmare 's analysis to learn more: https://www.dataforindia.com/ict-skills/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#ICT #Technology #DigitalIndia #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Two in three children under the age of five in India were anaemic as of 2021, as well as over half of women aged 15-49 years, according to the Nationa...
items.description_html:
Two in three children under the age of five in India were anaemic as of 2021, as well as over half of women aged 15-49 years, according to the National Family Health Survey (NFHS) 5th round.

Not only are India's anaemia numbers high, but NFHS data also suggests that the prevalence of anaemia in India rose between 2015-16 and 2019-21, the two most recent rounds of the survey. The prevalence is not restricted to poor states alone; Gujarat has the highest incidence of anaemia in children, and West Bengal has the highest among women.

India's high rates of anaemia are something of a puzzle in global health as during same period indicators like share of children and adults who are stunted, or the share of children and adults who receive adequate nutrition have shown improvement.

The answer may partly lie in how India collects data about anaemia.

Data on anaemia in India and many developing countries is collected using the capillary method, compared to the more sophisticated venous method. According to one recent systematic review, the capillary method that India uses produces higher and less reliable estimates.

The Indian government has already begun experimenting with a new survey using the venous method. Read @Rukmini 's piece to find out more: https://www.dataforindia.com/anaemia-in-india/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Children #Anaemia #Health #India #DataForIndia

items.title: During the 1970s, agriculture was the single biggest contributor to the Indian economy. About three-fourths of India's workers were engaged in agricul...
items.description_html:
During the 1970s, agriculture was the single biggest contributor to the Indian economy. About three-fourths of India's workers were engaged in agriculture, and the sector accounted for 40% of Gross Value Added to the country's economy.

Since then, a lot has changed for India's economy. Services have dominated India's economic shift, now accounting for over half of the GDP. But the jobs have not moved in quite the same way.

Agriculture dominates in all states, even the most industrialised ones. For instance, Maharashtra and Karnataka have 40% of the jobs in information technology and 30% of jobs in financial services. Yet, nearly half of the workers in these states are in agriculture.

The share of Indian workers in agriculture is higher than in countries with similar contexts, including China, Vietnam, Indonesia, Sri Lanka, and Bangladesh.

In recent decades, the services sub-sectors driving economic growth have employed far fewer people. Together, real estate, finance, professional services (including information technology, legal and accounting services) and public administration account for 28% of the economic output but just 6% of jobs.

#Agriculture #Economy #Employement #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Another failed critical mineral auction. Only ~48 per cent (24 out of 49) of the minerals blocks have been auctioned. A post on why that's the case. F...
items.description_html:
Another failed critical mineral auction. Only ~48 per cent (24 out of 49) of the minerals blocks have been auctioned.

A post on why that's the case.

First, remember the headlines from last year that one of the largest deposits of lithium ore in the world had been found in J&K’s Reasi district and that it could potentially transform India’s energy transition?

Well, it was just another case of "premature exhilaration" that we are pretty good at. Eighteen months later, that mineral block hasn’t attracted any interest in government-run auctions and remains unsold as of this writing. The more worrying part is that this isn’t a one-off failure. Turns out that India’s critical minerals drive isn’t going well: only 48 per cent of the minerals blocks on auction have gone through.

This situation isn’t explained by government neglect. If anything, policies to encourage the exploration and mining of critical minerals have rapidly improved over the last three years. In June last year, the government created a list of 30 critical and strategic minerals. Before that, a joint venture of three PSUs called Khanij Bidesh India Ltd. (KABIL) was incorporated to “identify and acquire overseas mineral assets of critical & strategic nature such as Lithium, Cobalt & others”.

In August 2023, the Parliament passed the Mines and Minerals (Development & Regulation) Amendment Bill, 2023, which paved the way for private companies to be involved in this domain. Hitherto, many of the critical minerals—including those of rare earth elements—were classified as ‘atomic minerals’ because some of them were found in monazite beach sands. They were thus closed off to the private sector and came under the purview of the Department of Atomic Energy. Through the 2023 amendment, six such minerals were taken off the ‘atomic minerals’ list. With this change, private sector companies could apply for licenses to explore and mine these minerals.

The second major change was the introduction of the ‘exploration license’. Many of these critical minerals are deep-seated in the earth, making their exploration difficult, time-consuming, and technically challenging. The International Energy Agency estimates that major global mining projects take nearly 16 years on average from discovery to production. The Act foresaw that mining firms would be sceptical of making such commitments. It hence introduced a separate license that permitted companies from across the world to participate only in reconnaissance and prospecting operations. Such exploration agencies would be reimbursed 50% of the exploration expenditure once mining starts. Essentially, a new category of players has been allowed to prevent vertical integration and to bring advanced exploration techniques to India.

This is a sound policy design. Nevertheless, the results have been discouraging. The National Mineral Exploration Trust‘s(NMET) website reveals that only a handful of exploration license projects for critical minerals such as Lithium, Rare Earth Elements, and Graphite have been green-lighted. That, too, most of these projects have been awarded to other PSUs, and I couldn’t locate a single foreign company in the mix. Further downstream, the mining license auctions have also been a dud. Despite multiple attempts, only 14 of the 38 blocks on auction have found any bidders thus far.

What Could’ve Gone Wrong?
Reports suggest that a major stumbling block is India's archaic resource classification standards. As the current standards do not require the exploration agencies to provide sufficient information on the economic viability of mining a mineral block, miners are wary of making any bids. Moreover, none of the blocks on auction hadn’t undergone detailed exploration, making bidders all the more circumspect. Here’s what an excellent Business Standard report says on this topic:

> According to the United Nations Framework for Classification of Resources, the exploration of minerals is divided into four stages — G4 (reconnaissance), G3 (prospecting), G2 (general exploration), and G1 (detailed exploration).Of the 38 auctioned blocks…none underwent G1 level exploration; 6 completed G2, 19 reached G3, and 13 were at G4. [Business Standard]

However, this explanation still doesn’t account for the lacklustre response to exploration licenses. The key insight is that exploration is the leverage point, not mining itself. The earlier that the government can get blocks through the various levels of exploration, the better the mining auction will be. So, let’s understand this stage better. From a public policy lens, the exploration stage resolves the market failure of information asymmetry. Here’s how.

In a well-functioning market for mineral rights, sellers (government) and buyers (mining companies) should have good information about what's being sold. This helps buyers value the blocks correctly and make informed bidding decisions. A lack of detailed exploration data creates a classic "market for lemons” problem: buyers can't distinguish good blocks from bad ones, and they must assume higher risk when valuing blocks. This further leads to lower bids than blocks might be worth, while some experienced companies stay away entirely.

The classic way to solve an information asymmetry problem is to regulate disclosure, like nutrition labels on packaged food products. But in this case, such regulations already exist in the form of resource classification standards (even if inadequately so).

So here’s an idea: the solution might be approaching critical minerals extraction as a semiconductor fabrication project. As with a fab, mining critical minerals is important strategically. More importantly, both these projects have long gestation periods and involve huge upfront capital investment. The Indian government solved this problem in chip fabrication by promising upfront capital support to fabs in the construction phase—companies don’t need to wait for production to begin before the government financial support kicks in. A similar approach could work with exploration license holders as well. The current incentive of reimbursing a firm after a mining project has begun doesn’t seem to be an attractive proposition for private and foreign firms. Upfront capital support for exploration would also rank favourably from a Marginal Cost of Public Finance (MCPF) perspective, as it would resolve a market failure and help unlock value many times over in downstream mining, exploration, sales, and exports.

Image source: @bsindia

items.title: India has an estimated 520 million women over the age of 14 who can legally work, but recent labour statistics show that only four in ten women are in...
items.description_html:
India has an estimated 520 million women over the age of 14 who can legally work, but recent labour statistics show that only four in ten women are in the labour force. India's female labour force participation rate (people who are either working or looking for work as a share of the population) is lower than in many countries at comparable levels of income and development.

As a result, women are significantly underrepresented in India's labour force. From an estimated 625 million-strong labour force, a little more than 215 million are women. The gender balance in the urban labour market is even more skewed. Urban India, where 500 million people live, has less than 50 million women in the labour force.

Women who are not in the labour force are still working, but in a way that is different from how work is traditionally understood. In Indian and international labour statistics, only those service activities that produce an output which can be sold are considered work. Most unpaid care and domestic work is performed by women.

Of the estimated 120 million men out of the labour force, the majority are in higher studies, while of the 300 million women who are out of the labour force, most are attending to "child care and personal commitments in home-making."

#Employment #WorkingWomen #FemaleLabourForceParticipation #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Infancy (the period before a child turns one) is a time of relatively high risk in a person's life. This risk is particularly pronounced in poorer reg...
items.description_html:
Infancy (the period before a child turns one) is a time of relatively high risk in a person's life. This risk is particularly pronounced in poorer regions of the world, where maternal undernutrition leads to birth disadvantages, and infants face a high risk of contracting communicable diseases.

As of 2020, India experienced 32 deaths for every 1,000 persons under the age of one. Half of all infant deaths in India occur within the baby's first week of life, and three in four take place within the first month.

Comparing across states shows the disproportionate risks to the very young in India's poorest states. The number of deaths per 1,000 persons under the age of one in Chhattisgarh are is more than three times that in Delhi, while in Uttar Pradesh, it is more than four times.

However, the risk of dying in infancy and early childhood has fallen dramatically in India over the last 70 years. In the 1950s, more than one in three deaths in India were of infants under the age of one. Deaths in early childhood now account for fewer than one in ten deaths in India.

#Mortality #Deaths #InfantDeaths #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Indian Cities: An Impossible Trinity The "What's the best Indian city?" flame wars are rather funny. All our cities are bad but just in different ways...
items.description_html:
Indian Cities: An Impossible Trinity

The "What's the best Indian city?" flame wars are rather funny. All our cities are bad but just in different ways. Some are worse than others.

Unless we recognise the problem, we will not search for solutions. So here is an impossible trinity to make sense of the problems with our cities.

The “impossible trinity” or the “policy trilemma” is a useful thinking aid. The framework is represented a choice among three favourable options, only two of which are possible at the same time. There’s nothing scientific about it, but it can help shed light on the trade-offs involved.

Living in many Indian cities can be represented as a trilemma between these three parameters:

1. A decent standard of living: means that a median resident can afford a dignified dwelling, can commute without fearing death or disability, and can breathe non-hazardous air most of the time.

2. Economic dynamism: means that the place offers a wide range of economic opportunities at all income levels. &

3. Individual liberty: means that a place allows an individual to be herself, where community beliefs do not suppress individual initiative, preferences, and expressions.

Some intentionally broad generalisations follow from this characterisation. Most of our smaller towns offer a reasonable standard of living but no economic dynamism and little individual liberty.

Places like Goa and perhaps cities in Kerala offer a decent standard of living and individual liberty but far fewer economic opportunities.

Cities such as Mumbai, Delhi, and Bengaluru offer economic dynamism and higher individual liberty but come at the expense of losing a decent standard of living.

Finally, we have a whole lot of cities which might offer you economic dynamism and a reasonable standard of living, but then they curtail one's basic freedoms - one has to modulate one's own food and drink habits due to others' concerns or look over one's shoulder constantly.

Does this trilemma make sense to you? And are there places that have resolved this impossible trinity? #cities #UrbanChallenges

items.title: The size of a country's economy is measured in terms of its gross domestic product (GDP). At $3.7 trillion in 2023, India's economy is the fifth large...
items.description_html:
The size of a country's economy is measured in terms of its gross domestic product (GDP). At $3.7 trillion in 2023, India's economy is the fifth largest in the world, and predicted to become the third largest by 2027. While GDP reflects the total size of a country's economy and indicates how big a market it represents, it does not, on its own, provide a sense of individual well-being.

One way to gauge individual well-being is by looking at per capita income, which is the GDP divided by the population. In 2023, while India ranked fifth among 200 countries in terms of total GDP, it was 158th out of 200 in terms of GDP per capita.

However, comparing countries this way can be tricky because the cost of goods and services varies significantly. For example, one US dollar can buy far more in rural India than in New York City. To address this, comparisons are often made using purchasing power parity (PPP), which creates a unit of measurement called the international dollar. This unit reflects the same purchasing power in any country.

In 2022, the UK’s per capita income in US dollar terms was 19 times that of India. However, in PPP terms, the UK’s per capita income was less than seven times India’s, revealing that a significant part of the gap is due to differences in price levels.

#Economy #GDP #PerCapitaIncome #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Last week, Data For India organised its first-person event, to discuss India’s evolving data ecosystem. The first session brought together leading wo...
items.description_html:
Last week, Data For India organised its first-person event, to discuss India’s evolving data ecosystem.

The first session brought together leading work from various organizations like @Agami_In , @Article14live , @daksh_india , @devdatalab , Project 39A, National Law University, Delhi and @Vidhi_India ; working on improving access to legal data across India. Speakers and participants shared their journeys and experiences, followed by an in-depth discussion around the eCourts data. The conversation also explored potential pathways to better understand and leverage data for the Indian legal system.

The second half featured distinguished speakers from government and industry. Dr. @_saurabhgarg from the @GoIStats (MOSPI), Mr. @abhish18 from the @GoI_MeitY (MeitY), and @dineshpaii from @zerodha and @Rainmatterin engaged in a panel discussion on India's public and private data landscape. The panel explored collaborative opportunities to build standards, provide training, and create access towards developing a robust data ecosystem for the country.

A big thank you to Prof. Anup Malani and the @UChicago Center in Delhi in helping us put together a successful event.

📨 Sign up to our newsletter for updates about future events! Link: https://www.dataforindia.com/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post&utm_campaign=event

items.title: With over 1.3 billion people, India is now the most populous country in the world. But since the 1990s, India's population growth has been gradually s...
items.description_html:
With over 1.3 billion people, India is now the most populous country in the world. But since the 1990s, India's population growth has been gradually slowing down. One reason behind this slowdown is the declining Total Fertility Rate (TFR), which represents the average number of children a woman is likely to have in her lifetime.

As countries become more economically developed and women gain better access to health and education, fertility rates tend to fall—a phenomenon observed globally. When a country's TFR drops to 2.1, demographers say that the country has reached 'replacement fertility'. Over the last half-century, India's TFR has slowly declined, dropping to 2.05 in 2020.

Beyond TFR, a country's population growth is also affected by the number of people who die every year. Until now, India's population growth slowdown has been powered by falling fertility alone. But in the future, rising mortality—or death rates—will play a role too. As India grows older, mortality rates will start to rise. By the mid-2060s, population growth is expected to turn negative—India will begin to have fewer people every year.

#Population #Census #PopulationData #India #DataForIndia

items.title: In 1998, India's Maternal Mortality Rate (MMR) stood at 398 deaths per 100,000 live births. By 2020, the rate dramatically reduced to 97 maternal deat...
items.description_html:
In 1998, India's Maternal Mortality Rate (MMR) stood at 398 deaths per 100,000 live births. By 2020, the rate dramatically reduced to 97 maternal deaths per 100,000 live births—a quarter of its previous figure. For context, the global average in 2020 stood at 223 maternal deaths per 100,000 live births.

One of the key factors in reducing maternal mortality in India has been the improvement in healthcare access for pregnant women. Adequate healthcare access like regular antenatal care (ANC) visits during pregnancy help identify and address potential complications early, and reducing potential delivery risks.

By 2021, over 93% of pregnant women had had at least one ANC visit, and 60% had four or more. The proportion of women not receiving any ANC visit dropped from 37% in 1993 to just 6% in 2021. However, challenges persist in the poorest rural households, where 15% still do not receive ANC during pregnancy.

Despite these remarkable strides, India remains some distance from the United Nations' Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 3.1, which aims to lower maternal mortality to fewer than 70 deaths per 100,000 live births by 2030.

#MaternalMortality #Pregnancy #India #Healthcare #DataForIndia

items.title: Around the world, 10 cows, 47 pigs, and 2,400 chickens get killed for meat every second. Recent estimates suggest that 99% of livestock in the United ...
items.description_html:
Around the world, 10 cows, 47 pigs, and 2,400 chickens get killed for meat every second.

Recent estimates suggest that 99% of livestock in the United States is factory-farmed. Globally, the majority of animals are raised on factory farms.

For many of these animals, life is short and painful.

Newborn calves are typically taken from their mothers shortly after birth, causing distress for both. Castrating and cutting off piglets' tails without anesthesia is common practice. Chickens grow so quickly that after six weeks, many struggle to stand for long and spend most of their time lying down.

These animals require high doses of antibiotics to survive in poor living conditions.

There are ways to make life less harsh for farm animals. Reducing meat consumption can lessen the demand for factory farming and its associated harms. Moreover, slowing chicken growth rates or giving hens better living spaces can help reduce their suffering.

These figures are based on estimates from the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by Simon van Teutem.)

items.title: Looking at literacy across age groups reveals a remarkable shift in India’s educational landscape over time. While more than half of those in their s...
items.description_html:
Looking at literacy across age groups reveals a remarkable shift in India’s educational landscape over time. While more than half of those in their sixties or older remain illiterate, illiteracy rates among those in their twenties are now in the single digits.

One significant contributor to this shift has been the closing of the gender gap in school enrolment. In 1951, fewer than 3 in 10 girls were enrolled in primary education, compared to 6 in 10 boys. At the upper primary level, girls were almost completely absent, with fewer than 5% enrolled. Today, the gap has been closed, and girls are now enrolled at nearly the same rate as boys at every educational stage.

Though illiteracy rates have dropped for both men and women, overall gender inequalities still exist, driven by the high rates of illiteracy among older women. In 2020, the illiteracy rate for women on average was almost the same as it was for men on average in the 1980s—showing that decades of progress still separate the genders in elementary education.

Additionally, socio-economic disparities still play a significant role, with educational gaps persisting amongst marginalised communities.

#Education #Literacy #EducationalAttainment #School #DataForIndia

items.title: In most places in the world, power from new renewables is now cheaper than power from new fossil fuels. Why did renewables become so cheap so fast? Th...
items.description_html:
In most places in the world, power from new renewables is now cheaper than power from new fossil fuels.

Why did renewables become so cheap so fast?

The answer: learning curves. For renewables and other technologies that follow learning curves, with each doubling of the cumulative installed capacity their price declines by the same fraction.

The price of electricity from fossil fuels, however, does not follow learning curves.

This means we should expect that the price difference between expensive fossil fuels and cheap renewables will become even larger in the future.

items.title: Over the last two decades, India has made consistent progress in improving access to sanitation, outpacing several countries that were at similar leve...
items.description_html:
Over the last two decades, India has made consistent progress in improving access to sanitation, outpacing several countries that were at similar levels or ahead at the time.

One key aspect of access to sanitation has been the use of toilets, and the decrease in open defecation. The proportion of Indians defecating in the open has dropped from 70% in 1993 to 19% by 2021. However, toilet usage varies significantly across states.

Northern and north-eastern states, record high toilet usage rates of over 90%, along with Kerala achieving 100%. Conversely, Bihar has the highest share of households not using any toilet facility at nearly 40%, followed by Jharkhand and Odisha, where around one in three households lack toilet facilities.

Over half the Indian households that are forced to defecate in the open reside in four states: Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Maharashtra, and Tamil Nadu.

#Toilets #RuralIndia #India #WashData #WASH #DataForIndia

items.title: Measles vaccination has saved an estimated 94 million lives globally since 1974. Of those, 92 million were children. The chart shows the number of liv...
items.description_html:
Measles vaccination has saved an estimated 94 million lives globally since 1974. Of those, 92 million were children.

The chart shows the number of lives saved by different childhood vaccines in the last 50 years. These estimates come from Andrew Shattock and colleagues and are based on global data on diseases, causes of death, vaccination rates, and vaccine efficacy.

Measles vaccines rank the highest in the total number of lives saved.

Measles is especially contagious and deadly. The virus depletes immune cells, making it harder to fight off measles and making other infections much more life-threatening. It can also erase immune memory to infections and vaccines that children have already encountered.

Before vaccines, almost all children caught measles, and it was a common cause of disability and death. With high measles vaccination rates, millions of lives are saved globally each year.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by Saloni Dattani.)

items.title: 1/7 According to the FT, "China’s long-term bond yields have fallen below Japan’s for the first time, as investors bet that the world’s second-bigg...
items.description_html:

items.description_html:
Over the last few years, the growth of India’s services exports has outstripped growth in goods exports, driven largely by India’s expanding software services export market. 👩🏽‍💻

India exported $150 billion worth of software or Information and Communication Technology (ICT) services in 2022-23, making it the biggest category, outdoing even petroleum products. In comparison India imported only $15 billion worth of software in the same year. Not only that, India now provides about 13% of global ICT service exports.

Apart from software which now accounts for more than half of India’s services exports, business services contribute nearly 25%.

#IndiaExports #Software #GlobalTrade #ICT #Economy #DataForIndia

items.title: In the early 2000s, less than 8/10 Indians had access to basic drinking water, lower than the world average and even our neighbours in Sri Lanka. Two ...
items.description_html:
In the early 2000s, less than 8/10 Indians had access to basic drinking water, lower than the world average and even our neighbours in Sri Lanka. Two decades later, nearly 95% of Indian households now have access to basic drinking water, a rate of progress that has surpassed the world's average.

However, when it comes to tap water, rural India still lags behind, with only 39% of households using it as their primary source of drinking water compared to 68% in urban regions. Hand pumps and tube wells remain the primary source of drinking water for nearly half of rural households. This reliance on hand pumps and tube wells in rural regions is especially pronounced in states like Uttar Pradesh (95%), Bihar (85%), and West Bengal (75%). 💧

Administrative data from a government scheme indicates that 60% of rural households have a tap water connection in their homes by the middle of 2023. The NSS survey data finds that only 30% of rural households used piped water in their own homes or yards as their primary source of drinking water as of 2023. 🔗

#Water #SurveyData #WashData #WASH #DataForIndia

items.title: Understanding who uses mobile phones in India and how they use them requires going beyond the data on registered SIM cards. While data on registered m...
items.description_html:
Understanding who uses mobile phones in India and how they use them requires going beyond the data on registered SIM cards.

While data on registered mobile SIM cards may hint at the scale of penetration, it does not capture how many people are using phones and who they are; some people and businesses may have more than one SIM card, while others may not be using phones at all. 📱

To understand how many people actually use phones and who they are, we looked at the latest data (released in Oct, 24) from a nationally representative sample survey of over a million people conducted by the Indian government about, among other things, their mobile phone and internet usage.

In 2023, 85% of adults or about 850 million people had used a mobile phone in the last three months. About 8/10 women and 9/10 men had used mobile phones recently. A majority of Indian phone users, particularly among women, share their phone with a family member, and are not exclusive users of the device they use. 🤝🏽 

Among those who do not use phones, is not a question of a lack of access. Nine in ten people who do not use a phone live in households that own a mobile phone.

#MobilePhone #Internet #Survey #GovernmentData #DataForIndia

items.title: While India boasts very successful elementary education enrolment, the country still faces challenges in enrolment for higher education. As of 2022, o...
items.description_html:
While India boasts very successful elementary education enrolment, the country still faces challenges in enrolment for higher education.

As of 2022, over 40 million young Indians (age 18-23) were enrolled in higher education programmes, but India’s enrolment rates is still below the global average. In the early 1990s, India and China had similar enrolment rates for both primary and higher education. However, in the decades since, China has made significant progress. Over 70% of young Chinese adults are enrolled in higher education, compared to just over 30% in India.

There are also sharp differences between states within India. Richer states have a higher proportion of graduates:
Kerala - Nearly 1 in 4 people hold a degree
Assam - Fewer than 1 in 20

#Education #HigherEducation #DataForIndia

items.title: Dal bhaat: Rajma ka madra with local sticky rice From Himachal Pradesh In episode 5 of the Green Kitchen, food historian and critic Professor Pushpesh...
items.description_html:
Dal bhaat: Rajma ka madra with local sticky rice From Himachal Pradesh
In episode 5 of the Green Kitchen, food historian and critic Professor Pushpesh Pant and CSE's Vibha Varshney discuss a rice and legume combination from Himachal Pradesh.
In Himachal, chickpeas, pulses, and rajma are usually cooked in Madra. Most of Himachali cuisine is sattvic and vegetarian, therefore, we're not even going to use onions or garlic today. Usually, Madra is served in a Dham or festive celebration. This is enjoyed with the local Sticky Rice.
Join Professor Pant as he delves into the history of this rice and beans variety along with their connection to the environment. Enjoy Dal bhaat - Rajma ka madra with local sticky rice with us!
The Green Kitchen is a show that celebrates the link between nature, nutrition and livelihood. It showcases foods sourced from local biodiversity
#food #nutrition #health #rice #rajma #himachalpradesh #Himachal #greenkitchen #downtoearth #research #foodshow

items.title: E2E became 25x today from the buying at 169. And, in all ways, it has been more surreal than I could ever dream of. I got in with a large position siz...
items.description_html:
E2E became 25x today from the buying at 169. And, in all ways, it has been more surreal than I could ever dream of. I got in with a large position size to begin with and the rally up in such a short time was sweet until one time but then it reminded me of countless experiences of the past in which I ended up giving humongous gains back just because I didn't sell. Honestly speaking, I had doubts about the company being able to raise funds and I am happy to be proven wrong. The sheer sentiments around compute is beyond euphoria. And just like it happens with everyone - what you sell goes up, what you don't, goes down. I have zero regrets on selling because I wanted to keep the gains and that's the largest ever I have made in my investing career in absolute terms. Cheers to those who joined along and made bumper returns.
More than money, it's the countless messages I receive each day, thanking for this and how many have made life changing returns, makes me happy beyond measures. But I give much credit to luck too. Buying is in our hands, but when and how much it goes up isn't. Cheers to everyone and Wishing all a very happy, healthy and prosperous Diwali 🎇 🪔

items.title: People tend to think there are more immigrants in their country than there really are. In the United States, for example, the average person believes ...
items.description_html:
People tend to think there are more immigrants in their country than there really are.

In the United States, for example, the average person believes that 33% of people were not born there. However, official estimates from government agencies show that the figure is just 15% — less than half as many.

In Japan, people guess that 10% of the population are immigrants, but the accurate figure is around 2%. For every 5 immigrants people think exist, there is only one in reality.

As the chart shows, this mismatch is common everywhere. The data comes from the 2023 Ipsos Perils of Perception report.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by Simon van Teutem.)

items.title: How far apart are the incomes of the rich and poor in different countries? The chart shows how incomes are spread in several countries in different wo...
items.description_html:
How far apart are the incomes of the rich and poor in different countries?

The chart shows how incomes are spread in several countries in different world regions. The data comes from the excellent Luxembourg Income Study.

Blue points show the monthly after-tax income of someone who falls just inside the richest 10% of their country's population. Red points show the income of someone who falls just inside the poorest 10%.

To allow for comparisons, all incomes are shown in international dollars, which account for differences in cost of living across countries.

The ratio between these two numbers gives us a measure of inequality known as the “P90/P10 ratio”.

In Norway, a country with very low inequality, this ratio is around 3. A person just inside the richest 10% has a monthly income of $5,490 — a little more than 3 times the $1,760 earned by someone just inside the poorest 10%.

In the United States, inequality is much higher, and the ratio is twice as large — around 6. The chart shows that the richest 10% are much richer than those in Norway, with incomes above $7,440. But, the poorest are also poorer, with incomes of less than $1,240.

In many countries, the ratio is between 4 and 6. But in the most unequal countries, it is much higher.

In South Africa, the ratio is 22. Those in the bottom decile are among the poorest people in the world, living on less than $110 per month. In contrast, the richest 10% earn $2,490 or above — higher than the incomes of half the UK population and nearly a third of the US population.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by @JoeHasell.)

items.title: New petrol and diesel cars are becoming a rarity in Norway. In 2023, 93% of new cars sold in the country were electric. This is shown in the chart, ba...
items.description_html:
New petrol and diesel cars are becoming a rarity in Norway.

In 2023, 93% of new cars sold in the country were electric. This is shown in the chart, based on data from the International Energy Agency.

This is a rapid increase from a decade ago when just 6% of new cars were electric.

Here, “electric cars” include fully battery-electric and plug-in hybrid cars (which have a smaller battery and also have a combustion engine).

But in Norway, battery-electric cars now dominate: in 2023, 85% were fully electric, compared to just 8% of plug-in hybrids.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie.)

items.title: Chile produced 9.4% of its primary energy from solar in 2023 — the highest in any country that year. According to the Energy Institute’s Statistical...
items.description_html:
Chile produced 9.4% of its primary energy from solar in 2023 — the highest in any country that year.

According to the Energy Institute’s Statistical Review of World Energy, in 2023, Chile produced 9.4% of its primary energy from solar sources, the highest share in any country. When we look at electricity alone, solar produced 20% of the total.

This marks a trend of continued year-on-year growth in a country that, just a decade earlier, generated almost no electricity from solar.

Chile's growth has been faster than that of other solar champions like Spain and Australia, where the adoption of these technologies started earlier.

Much of Chile's solar energy is captured in the Atacama Desert. This region, in Northern Chile, receives the highest level of sunlight exposure in the world and is home to Latin America’s first solar thermal plant.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by @EOrtizOspina.)

items.title: New @mercatus Policy Paper Alert: 📢 Did you know that 95% of India’s industrial units employ fewer than 10 workers. One of the main reasons Indian...
items.description_html:
New @mercatus Policy Paper Alert: 📢

Did you know that 95% of India’s industrial units employ fewer than 10 workers. One of the main reasons Indian firms are reluctant to scale is prohibitively costly labor regulation.

In this paper, @kadambari_shah and I survey India's labor laws, and find that most labor regulation is too onerous, tends to micromanage factory operations through uncertain enforcement by a labor inspection system, preventing firms from remaining competitive. Second, most of the laws are imposed on firms with as few as 10 employees, disincentivizing firm growth and large-scale employment.

TL;DR: India's labor laws are do too much, too soon in a firm’s life cycle.

We recommend streamlining labor regulations, removing outdated laws, overhauling the inspection system, and judiciously applying criminal penalties. While these reforms contain various political economy challenges, a second-best alternative could be increasing thresholds of existing laws to 1,000 workers for most labor laws and 10,000 for industrial disputes to foster large-scale firms, boost India’s manufacturing sector, and enhance its global competitiveness.

https://the1991project.com/writing/papers/why-indian-firms-dont-scale-labor-edition

items.title: Vaccines account for 40% of the decline in infant mortality over the last 50 years. Infant mortality rates have plummeted over the last 50 years. Glob...
items.description_html:
Vaccines account for 40% of the decline in infant mortality over the last 50 years.

Infant mortality rates have plummeted over the last 50 years. Globally, they’ve fallen by over two-thirds, from around 10% in 1974 to less than 3% today.

A recent study — published in The Lancet by Andrew Shattock and an international team of researchers — estimates that 40% of this decline has been driven by increased access to crucial vaccines.

The chart shows the actual reduction in infant mortality rates with vaccination (in blue) and the researchers’ estimates for a hypothetical scenario in which vaccines wouldn’t have been rolled out (in red).

Based on these figures, vaccines are estimated to have saved 150 million children over the last 50 years.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie.)

items.title: The average worker in wealthy countries now works only about half as many hours per year as in the late 19th century. Based on data from economic hist...
items.description_html:
The average worker in wealthy countries now works only about half as many hours per year as in the late 19th century.

Based on data from economic historians Michael Huberman and Chris Minns, the average worker used to work between 2,700 and 3,500 hours per year, which is about 50 to 70 hours each week.

Recently, using data from the Penn World Table, workers worked about half that amount, between 1,300 and 1,800 hours a year, or about 25 to 35 hours a week.

This decrease has come from working fewer hours each day, fewer days each week, and fewer weeks each year.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by @bbherre.)

items.title: There are two ways to produce seafood: catch fish in the wild or farm your own. Seafood farming is often called “aquaculture”. Aquaculture is domina...
items.description_html:
There are two ways to produce seafood: catch fish in the wild or farm your own. Seafood farming is often called “aquaculture”. Aquaculture is dominated by the farming of fish, but also includes other organisms, such as crustaceans and aquatic plants.

Aquaculture has grown rapidly over the last few decades. In fact, as the chart shows, it has overtaken wild catch since 2013.

This has relieved some pressure on wild fish stocks: if this increased demand for fish had been satisfied by wild catch, then many more would be severely overexploited.

(This Daily Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie.)

items.title: In 1820, only 1 in 10 people over the age of 15 could read. Today, the corresponding global literacy rate — the share of adults aged 15 and older who...
items.description_html:
In 1820, only 1 in 10 people over the age of 15 could read. Today, the corresponding global literacy rate — the share of adults aged 15 and older who can read and write — is 87%. That means more than 5 billion people can read and write today, compared to fewer than 100 million two centuries ago.

The chart shows the rising global literacy rate over time. The data combines historical estimates from academic historians with more recent statistics from UNESCO.

As we can see, widespread literacy is a recent achievement. This trend underlines a huge achievement, but it’s important to remember that there’s still much progress ahead of us. In many schools, children learn very little, and many still do not attend school.

This Data Insight was written by @EOrtizOspina.

items.title: According to the most recent data from the Energy Institute’s Statistical Review of World Energy, India now consumes more coal than the continents of...
items.description_html:
According to the most recent data from the Energy Institute’s Statistical Review of World Energy, India now consumes more coal than the continents of Europe and North America combined.

The chart shows this was not the case until recently. Coal consumption in Europe and North America was high for a long time but has significantly decreased in recent decades. At the same time, India’s consumption has steadily increased.

India has industrialized and is growing rapidly. It has a huge demand for cheap energy, and the country’s abundant coal reserves are being used to meet it.

On a per-capita basis, coal consumption in India has only just passed levels in either region. That’s after centuries of higher consumption in North America and Europe.

(This Data Insight was written by @EOrtizOspina and Pablo Rosado.)

items.title: Energy demand in China has increased rapidly over the last few decades due to rising incomes and industrialization. The country now uses about the sam...
items.description_html:
Energy demand in China has increased rapidly over the last few decades due to rising incomes and industrialization.

The country now uses about the same amount of energy per person as the European Union. You can see this in this chart, with new data from the Energy Institute’s Statistical Review of World Energy.

This measure of primary energy is based on the substitution method, which tries to account for the inefficiencies of fossil fuels compared to renewables.

Note that this does not account for energy embedded in traded goods, so some of this increase in China has come from producing goods exported to other countries.

(This Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie and Pablo Rosado.)

items.title: According to the latest Global Burden of Disease Study — published last month by the Institute of Health Metrics and Evaluation (IHME) — COVID-19 wa...
items.description_html:
According to the latest Global Burden of Disease Study — published last month by the Institute of Health Metrics and Evaluation (IHME) — COVID-19 was the third leading cause of death in 2021, after cardiovascular diseases and cancer.

These estimates suggest that COVID-19 was responsible for around eight million deaths in 2021. In many countries across South America and sub-Saharan Africa, the IHME reports that it was the leading cause of death.

Global improvements in healthcare have led to a steady reduction in the death rate from infectious diseases in recent decades, but the COVID-19 pandemic has reversed this trend.

(This Data Insight was written by
@f_spooner.)

items.title: One of the most transformative changes in technology over the last few decades has been the massive drop in the cost of clean energy. Solar photovolta...
items.description_html:
One of the most transformative changes in technology over the last few decades has been the massive drop in the cost of clean energy. Solar photovoltaic costs have fallen by 90% in the last decade, onshore wind by 70%, and batteries by more than 90%.

These technologies have followed a “learning curve” called Wright’s Law. This states that the cost of technology falls consistently as the cumulative production of that technology increases.

The chart shows the perfect example of this for solar power. This data comes from the International Renewable Agency, Greg Nemet, and Doyne Farmer & François Lafond.

On the horizontal axis, we have the cumulative installed capacity of solar panels, and on the vertical axis, the cost. Both are measured on logarithmic scales, and the trend follows a straight line. That means the fall in cost has been exponential.

Costs have fallen by around 20% every time the global cumulative capacity doubles. Over four decades, solar power has transformed from one of the most expensive electricity sources to the cheapest in many countries.

(This Data Insight was written by
@_HannahRitchie)

items.title: Great concall by #E2ENetworks. After a pause last Q, it was great to hear from the promoters. Some of the key points I noted: 1. The first is capex fo...
items.description_html:
Great concall by #E2ENetworks. After a pause last Q, it was great to hear from the promoters. Some of the key points I noted:

1. The first is capex for FY25. Plan is to do 800cr capex which will be funded through a combination of banking financing, vendor financing & equity raise. This capex represents the incremental demand company sees and notices no risk in paying off the debt, if any

2. A lot of capex came in stream and current quarter's MRR has some contribution from that additional capex. However, in next 2 Qs the additional capex will fully come in and with this management is confident of clocking 14-16cr of MRR. And their capacity, including additional capex, is fully booked already!

3. Let me clear this once - The co is primarily focused on India but they will eventually server EMEA & US markets (remember they run training & inference on a single core because of this market focus, we talked about this in last concall)

4. The outage (a decadal black swan event as per management) was caused by the vendor who hosts their infra. The vendor team started with UPS without being fully charged when there was a power outage and this caused power surge and damaged some equipment. However, before SLA, co restored the service since they have additional infra on site

5. Was good to see one of their clients join the call & appreciating their service

In sum, as I have been saying this all along, this is never about the infra. And management echoes this. Nobody buys a raw GPU anymore. You need a while SW ecosystem around. Nobody can just go buy a GPU and say let me compete in open market. This is where #E2E has the edge. It remains a SW driven compute provider!

Deaths have declined not because disasters are now less frequent or intense, but because we’ve become better at protecting ourselves and each other.

items.title: Good set of results from E2E.. Growth Growth Growth is what this result shows all around.. 1. Quarterly revenue growth of 23% in Q4 vs an avg of 10-11...
items.description_html:
Good set of results from E2E.. Growth Growth Growth is what this result shows all around..

1. Quarterly revenue growth of 23% in Q4 vs an avg of 10-11% qoq growth in the first 3 qoq results..

2. ⁠Company has done a massive capex of ~140-150 cr in H2 (most of it coming in Q4, as suggested by the huge increase in Q4 depreciation number)..

3. Typically company runs at an asset turn of 60-70%, won’t be surprised if company adds around 90cr revenue in FY25 and reaches 180-190 cr revenue number in FY25 itself..

4. ⁠A part of the capex was financed using ~100 cr of debt, therefore the increase in finance cost in Q4..

5. ⁠From next quarter expecting a significant increase in revenues as this massive capex comes online (while the growth in dep, interest cost and other expenses will be at a much lower %, as Q4 results factors in most of these increased costs)..

Expecting 180 cr revenue and atleast 90 cr EBITDA in FY25.. Stock trades around 17 times EV/EBITDA FY25..

Get ready for massive revenue growth ahead!!

Tons of credits to my partner in scheme of things @saumyakothari91 for a detailed analysis.

items.title: No point in trying to guess why Musk visited China and not India. And that's a wrong question to ask. What we should take away is that such setbacks a...
items.description_html: No point in trying to guess why Musk visited China and not India. And that's a wrong question to ask. What we should take away is that such setbacks are a feature of pro-business policies (rather than pro-market ones). In the former, govt is spending time & enery in designing policies that have the pain points of a couple of anchor investors in mind. That always runs the risk of those investors not showing interest. Instead, we probably need to address the structural bottlenecks that stop all kinds of investors and businesses from investing in India. @anupammanur

items.title: Amidst all the gloom & crash posts let me just share my experience & learnings of 2008!! Back then the crash was very prolonged & painful. I was new i...
items.description_html:
Amidst all the gloom & crash posts let me just share my experience & learnings of 2008!! Back then the crash was very prolonged & painful. I was new in the market & left market after losing major capital. I came back only after 3yrs. The reason was two fold:
1. Many companies I invested in hardly had any good fundamentals.
2. Some companies were very good though
And, I treated everything the same, panicked.
My father also sold his MF holding in hurry. I stood no chance of recovering because companies lacked strong business but my father's MF holding could have grown multi-fold had he just stayed in.

Long story short, if you are holding good businesses, the only way to fight this crash & win is - staying in! If you've got the 1st act right - good business with good management, just stay in.

If you are holding bad companies, this is a lesson & is definitely not the end of the road! I felt like back in 2008 but now 16yrs later it doesn't feel like. So just staying the course is best solution. Stay positive, learn where possible, and just stay in. You can always make a come back!

items.title: Nearly 1,000 offices of diamond companies are likely to wind up operations in Mumbai and relocate to the Surat Diamond Bourse (SDB) Billionaire diamon...
items.description_html:
Nearly 1,000 offices of diamond companies are likely to wind up operations in Mumbai and relocate to the Surat Diamond Bourse (SDB)

Billionaire diamond trader Vallabhbhai Lakhani, Kiran Gems, has set the ball rolling by moving his Rs 17,000 Cr business to Surat, and developing a mini-township for his employees

Remember reading in the Ken that the bourse is offering lot of relaxations on membership fees and one source also mentioned that the restaurant and tapri/small stall owners nearby were also promised shops if they shift to Surat!

Poaching going on in full force and from a cost and space perspective also it makes sense!

Mumbai needs to keep up with the Infra or else it will keep on losing out!

items.title: If you are 20 and start making a monthly SIP of Rs 3184, you will reach 10 Cr corpus at 60 years. But if you waste time till you reach 35, you will ne...
items.description_html:
If you are 20 and start making a monthly SIP of Rs 3184, you will reach 10 Cr corpus at 60 years.

But if you waste time till you reach 35, you will need to do a monthly sip of Rs 30450 (almost 10x) to reach the 10 cr mark.

The message to youngsters is - Experiment, take your risks while you are young, but keep aside your SIP without any compromise.

Also if you start at 35, you will need to set invest a total sum of 91.3 lakhs against investment of 15.28 lakhs by someone who started at 20.

This is the power and magic of compounding when age is on your side.

items.title: If you are bullish on India, you should be double bullish on Uttar Pradesh. As per RBI August bulletin, UP has the highest share of projects financed ...
items.description_html:
If you are bullish on India, you should be double bullish on Uttar Pradesh. As per RBI August bulletin, UP has the highest share of projects financed by Banks in FY22-23 at 16.2%. This was just 1.1% a decade back!

UP has 20% of India's population. This is more than the total population of all but 5 countries in the world! When you imagine the scale and the base effect, you will see massive investment opportunities.

Here's how an investor can approach and play this:

1. Figure out which Infra and building materials companies are strong in UP and doing projects. Eg: Why PNC Infratech is strong in UP, which companies are doing steel pipe and gas pipe layout?

2. There were ~12 lakh ITRs filed this year vs 1.4 lakh in 2014. So income levels are rising and consumption will rise more. Figure out which consumption oriented companies are strong in UP. Apparel, footwear, alcohol, etc.

3. NBFCs and MFIs which are strong in UP. A lot of people will be setting own small businesses. Women setting up small stores, etc. Which lender is catering to them?

I feel the first stage of UP's progress was laid in bringing law and order and hard measures wherever needed. I don't want to bring any political bias, but there's a reason why Yogi Adityanath is considered the most important minister in BJP. Now that UP is moving from lawlessness to progress, it will steer a large part of India's growth. Many companies will go from 500 crore to 20000 crore mcap in next 5-10 years.

As an investor, our job is to keep biases aside and focus on making the right investments. Let's just focus on that.

Met my college friends in Hyderabad who are putting up a manufacturing facility in one of the SEZs in the region

They said they were treated like VIPs at the land bank office and all of their queries were solved quickly

They said they never felt so happy being entrepreneurs - which was amazing to hear.

Governments who are extending such quick help and incentives to entrepreneurs will definitely bring about a change in their respective states

I do see some states who are in a war footing to garner more investments - this is so nice to see and a welcome change!

items.title: Price caps on 50 per cent of private college medical seats is a terrible policy. It will lead to fewer and smaller private colleges. The fee of the re...
items.description_html:
Price caps on 50 per cent of private college medical seats is a terrible policy. It will lead to fewer and smaller private colleges. The fee of the remaining 50 per cent will rise further. Instead of increasing the total number of seats, we will end up creating more scarcity.

हमने तय किया है कि प्राइवेट मेडिकल कॉलेजों में आधी सीटों पर सरकारी मेडिकल कॉलेज के बराबर ही फीस लगेगी: PM @narendramodi

Laurus Labs : A Visual Story

I am a Data Science / Machine Learning developer by profession and data along with finance are my two areas of competence.

I realize how powerful combining both of them can be, so here is a visual analysis for Laurus Labs.

Now we have very few sellers and even with few buyers stocks skyrocket. Imbalances not flows create extremes.

items.title: Today you may tag me for your winning stocks. Tomorrow you may blame me for your losers. It’s your risk/reward game. Play it your way. Remember it’s...
items.description_html:
Today you may tag me for your winning stocks. Tomorrow you may blame me for your losers. It’s your risk/reward game. Play it your way. Remember it’s always your capital at risk. Your conviction. Your goals.

Kindly refrain from excessive flattery and tagging. Thanks.


Antifragile Thinking: Humble request - please don't shower any credit (or discredit) for interim gains (or losses) in your holdings. You know your personal goals. You know your #risk profile. You know what you're doing. You're mature enough to act sensibly. Refrain from instant gratification. Thanks.

Early this morning Great Britain's electricity system passed a new coal-free record of 18 days, 6 hours & 15 minutes.

With demand down 18% due to #coronavirus, supply has been met by:

37% renewables
32% gas
22% nuclear
9% imports

https://www.carbonbrief.org/analysis-great-britain-hits-coal-free-electricity-record-amid-coronavirus-lockdown
